proposals.title: Lump, Soliton, and Traveling Wave Solutions for (3+1)-Dimensional Integrable Systems with Time-Delay Arguments
proposals.motivation: Time-delay terms are crucial for modeling real-world phenomena with finite signal propagation speeds, such as in biological systems, optical fibers, and neural networks. However, pre-2026 analytical methods for exact solutions, like the Hirota bilinear method and symbolic computation used by Wazwaz, have been predominantly applied to instantaneous nonlinear evolution equations. Extending these powerful integrability-based techniques to delay differential equations presents a significant and timely challenge in mathematical physics.
proposals.hypothesis: That the established frameworks for constructing multiple soliton, lump, and traveling wave solutions for (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations can be systematically generalized to incorporate constant time-delay arguments, leading to new classes of delayed soliton solutions with modified interaction properties.
proposals.method: We will first incorporate a constant delay parameter τ into the nonlinear terms of established (3+1)-dimensional integrable models, such as the combined pKP-BKP or an extended fifth-order equation. The core approach will be to adapt the Hirota bilinear method for delay equations by seeking bilinear forms with delayed dependent variables. We will then algorithmically apply symbolic computation (e.g., in Maple or Mathematica) to derive delayed N-soliton solutions via perturbative expansions and to construct lump solutions using positive quadratic functions in delayed variables. The dynamics of these solutions will be analyzed graphically.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first explicit analytical constructions of lump and multi-soliton solutions for high-dimensional integrable systems with time delay. It will establish a methodological bridge between soliton theory and delay differential equations, providing new benchmark solutions for testing numerical schemes and potentially explaining delayed coherent structures in applied sciences.
proposals.novelty: While delay equations are studied numerically, the direct analytical extension of the Hirota bilinear formalism and lump solution ansatz to (3+1)-dimensional integrable delay systems is a novel and non-trivial advancement beyond pre-2026 soliton theory, which largely ignored explicit time-delay terms in such high-dimensional contexts.

proposals.title: Probing Non-Integrable (3+1)-Dimensional Systems: A Systematic Test of Analytical Solution Methods for Equations with Unknown Exact Solutions
proposals.motivation: The vast majority of nonlinear evolution equations (NLEEs) arising in physics and engineering are non-integrable, yet their analytical study is crucial for understanding complex wave dynamics. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Wazwaz's work, has heavily focused on deriving exact solutions for known integrable equations, leaving a gap in rigorously testing the power and limitations of advanced ansatz methods on systems where the solution is not known a priori. This project addresses this gap by applying established soliton solution techniques to complex, physically motivated equations whose integrability status and exact solutions are unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the systematic application of a combined analytical toolkit—including the Hirota bilinear method, simplified Hirota's method, and the Cole-Hopf transformation—can yield novel, verifiable analytical solutions (e.g., multi-wave, lump, periodic) for select non-integrable (3+1)-dimensional NLEEs, even when the exact solution is not pre-known, thereby extending the practical utility of these methods beyond the integrable domain.
proposals.method: We will select two candidate (3+1)-dimensional NLEEs from fluid dynamics and nonlinear optics with unclear integrability. The core technical approach involves a sequential, algorithmic application of established pre-2026 methods: 1) Use the Painlevé integrability test as a preliminary filter. 2) For non-integrable candidates, apply the Cole-Hopf transformation to reduce the equation's order. 3) Employ the Hirota bilinear method with a generalized ansatz for N-soliton solutions. 4) Utilize the simplified Hirota's direct method and extended trial function schemes to search for lump, breather, and interaction solutions. All derived solutions will be verified by direct substitution into the original equation using symbolic computation (e.g., Maple).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical benchmark for the applicability of exact solution methods to non-integrable systems, potentially uncovering new classes of solutions for physically relevant models. It will contribute a structured methodology for tackling 'hard' problems and yield concrete solution sets that can serve as benchmarks for numerical simulations and perturbation studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in developing new methods, but in the deliberate, rigorous, and comparative application of a suite of established pre-2026 analytical methods to equations chosen specifically for their unknown solution profiles, moving beyond the standard practice of applying these methods primarily to known integrable hierarchies.

proposals.title: A Symbolic-Numeric Framework for Soliton and Traveling Wave Solutions in Large-Scale Coupled Nonlinear ODE Systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the analysis of large-scale coupled nonlinear ODE systems, prevalent in fields like complex network dynamics, quantum lattices, and biological oscillators, remains a significant challenge. While powerful methods exist for single equations or small (2+1)-dimensional PDEs, scaling these techniques to high-dimensional ODE systems is computationally prohibitive and lacks systematic symbolic approaches. This gap limits our ability to find analytical solution structures like solitons and traveling waves in such complex systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The symbolic integrability and solution construction techniques developed for (3+1)-dimensional integrable PDEs can be algorithmically adapted and combined with numerical continuation methods to efficiently derive and characterize localized wave solutions in large-scale, coupled nonlinear ODE systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid symbolic-numeric algorithm. First, we extend the Painlevé integrability test and Hirota's bilinear method to symbolically identify subsystems or collective variables within a large ODE network that admit exact solutions (e.g., multiple solitons, lumps). This symbolic reduction will be automated using computer algebra. Second, for non-integrable or high-dimensional cases, we will employ numerical wave-ansatz continuation methods, using the symbolic output as robust initial seeds, to trace traveling wave and soliton-like solutions across parameter spaces and network couplings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, practical toolkit for extracting analytical solution patterns from large nonlinear ODE systems, bridging symbolic integrability theory and applied numerical analysis. It will advance the field by demonstrating how soliton dynamics manifest in discrete, high-dimensional settings, with direct implications for modeling wave propagation in engineered and biological networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic translation of PDE-based integrability and soliton solution techniques to the domain of large-scale ODE systems, a non-trivial scaling that moves beyond ad-hoc or purely numerical approaches to offer structured analytical insight.

proposals.title: Lie Symmetry Analysis, Symmetry Reductions, and New Analytic Solutions for the Painlevé Integrable (3+1)-Dimensional Estevez-Prada Equation
proposals.motivation: The (3+1)-dimensional Estevez-Prada equation, recently shown to possess Painlevé integrability, is a significant model for studying nonlinear wave phenomena in higher dimensions. While multiple soliton solutions have been constructed, a systematic Lie symmetry analysis to uncover its full group-invariant structure and derive novel reductions remains unexplored. Applying this foundational method will provide a deeper understanding of its integrability and generate solution classes beyond standard solitons and traveling waves.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the application of Lie group theory will reveal a non-trivial symmetry algebra for the Estevez-Prada equation, enabling its reduction to lower-dimensional ordinary differential equations (ODEs) whose solutions correspond to new, physically meaningful wave patterns not obtainable via direct ansatz methods.
proposals.method: We will employ the classical Lie symmetry algorithm. First, we will compute the vector fields of the symmetry Lie algebra by applying the infinitesimal generator to the equation and solving the resulting determining system. Using the admitted symmetries, we will systematically perform symmetry reductions to transform the PDE into (2+1)-, (1+1)-dimensional PDEs, and ultimately into ODEs. These ODEs will be solved analytically using integration, the (G'/G)-expansion method, or by mapping to known integrable equations, yielding invariant solutions including similarity, periodic, and rational forms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the complete Lie point symmetry classification and optimal system of subalgebras for the Estevez-Prada equation, a foundational result. It will yield new classes of exact solutions, such as similarity solutions and group-invariant wave structures, enriching the known solution manifold. The analysis will further corroborate the equation's integrability from a group-theoretic perspective.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in conducting the first comprehensive Lie symmetry study for this specific Painlevé-integrable (3+1)D equation, moving beyond solution construction via ansatz to a systematic group-theoretic framework. The derived similarity and invariant solutions will be distinct from the previously reported solitons and traveling waves.

proposals.title: Approximate Analytical Solutions for Non-Integrable Seventh-Order KdV Equations via the Adomian Decomposition Method
proposals.motivation: Higher-order Korteweg-de Vries (KdV) equations, such as the KdV7, model complex nonlinear wave phenomena in physics and engineering. While integrable forms have been extensively studied, non-integrable variants are less tractable, lacking exact soliton solutions. Pre-2026, there is a recognized need for robust semi-analytical methods to construct reliable approximate solutions for these challenging non-integrable models, which are crucial for understanding wave dynamics beyond perturbative regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: The Adomian Decomposition Method (ADM) can be effectively adapted to generate convergent series solutions for physically relevant non-integrable KdV7-type equations, providing accurate analytical approximations for solitary wave profiles and their interactions.
proposals.method: We will apply the standard ADM algorithm to a canonical non-integrable KdV7 equation, u_t + α u u_x + β u_{3x} + γ u_{5x} + δ u_{7x} = 0. The nonlinear term u u_x will be decomposed into a series of Adomian polynomials A_n. The solution u(x,t) will be constructed as an infinite series ∑_{n=0}^∞ u_n(x,t), where the components u_n are determined recursively from the integral form of the equation. The convergence of the series will be analyzed theoretically and validated numerically against direct simulations for specific initial conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic application of ADM to seventh-order non-integrable KdV equations, yielding benchmark approximate solutions. It will extend the analytical toolkit for nonlinear wave theory and offer a verifiable method for researchers studying high-dimensional, non-integrable systems where exact techniques fail.
proposals.novelty: While ADM has been applied to lower-order KdV equations, its targeted application to non-integrable KdV7 models is novel. This bridges Dr. Wazwaz's expertise in exact solitons for integrable systems with powerful approximation methods for non-integrable ones, a less explored synergy pre-2026.

proposals.title: Formulation and Analysis of the Inverse Scattering Transform for Integrable Seventh-Order KdV Equations
proposals.motivation: The seventh-order KdV equations, such as the Kaup-Kupershmidt and Sawada-Kotera-Ito equations, are well-known integrable hierarchies with rich multi-soliton and traveling wave structures, as studied extensively pre-2026. While direct methods for constructing solutions exist, a complete formulation of the corresponding Inverse Scattering Transform (IST) problem—the cornerstone of integrable systems theory—remains an open and challenging task for these higher-order cases. Establishing the IST framework is crucial for a rigorous understanding of their solution manifolds, long-time asymptotics, and full nonlinear superposition principles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Lax pairs for integrable seventh-order KdV equations can be used to formulate a well-posed Riemann-Hilbert problem on the complex spectral plane, and that the associated scattering data possesses a specific symmetry structure enabling the reconstruction of multi-soliton and localized wave solutions.
proposals.method: The approach will first derive and analyze the asymptotic behavior of Jost solutions for the spatial part of the known Lax pair. We will then define the scattering coefficients and establish their analytic properties and symmetries, leveraging the equation's hereditary characteristics. The core technical step is to formulate the inverse problem as a matrix Riemann-Hilbert problem. Finally, we will apply the dressing method and pole analysis to this Riemann-Hilbert framework to explicitly recover known soliton solutions and investigate the potential for generating novel rational (lump-type) and breather solutions in the (1+1)-dimensional setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic IST formulation for a class of seventh-order integrable KdV equations, filling a significant gap in the theory of high-order integrable systems. It will rigorously connect their Lax integrability to solution-generation mechanisms, paving the way for future studies on perturbation theory, numerical IST algorithms, and possible extensions to (2+1)- and (3+1)-dimensional analogues relevant to the researcher's recent work.
proposals.novelty: While the IST for the standard KdV equation is classical, its extension to the seventh-order members of the hierarchy is non-trivial due to the higher-order spectral problem and more complicated scattering data structure; this proposal moves beyond direct solution construction methods to address this foundational inverse problem.

proposals.title: Beyond Traveling Waves: Constructing Novel Exact Solutions for (3+1)-Dimensional Integrable Equations via Generalized Ansatz and Hirota Bilinear Methods
proposals.motivation: The vast majority of exact solutions reported for (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations, such as the pKP–BKP and Estevez-Prada models, rely on the traveling wave ansatz u = U(z), where z is a linear combination of variables. This assumption, while productive, severely restricts the solution space, potentially overlooking rich dynamical structures like breathers, localized lumps with non-uniform motion, and resonant soliton complexes that are not co-linear or stationary in a moving frame. Exploring solutions beyond this standard form is crucial for a complete understanding of the dynamical systems described by these equations.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing a more general, multi-parameter ansatz that decouples the functional dependence on spatial and temporal coordinates, novel families of exact solutions—including non-traveling lumps, periodic-in-time breathers, and interacting wave patterns with time-dependent velocities—can be systematically constructed for key (3+1)-dimensional Painlevé-integrable equations.
proposals.method: The research will employ the Hirota bilinear method, a cornerstone of Wazwaz's recent work. The core technical innovation is to replace the standard ansatz for the auxiliary function F in the bilinear form. Instead of F = 1 + exp(η) with η = kx + ly + mz - ωt, we will use a generalized form such as F = 1 + f(x, y, t) + g(z, t), or F = 1 + A(t) exp(p(t)x + q(t)y) + B(t) exp(r(t)z). This introduces time-dependent parameters and decouples dependencies. The resulting bilinear equations will be solved algebraically by balancing coefficients of independent exponential and polynomial terms, leading to constraints on the introduced functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will systematically generate and classify new, non-traveling wave exact solutions for several important (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations. It will expand the known solution libraries, provide explicit examples of more complex dynamical behaviors directly from the equations, and offer new test functions for numerical and stability analyses. The methodology will serve as a template for relaxing the traveling wave constraint in other integrable systems.
proposals.novelty: While generalized ansatzes exist for (1+1)-dimensions, their application to the more complex bilinear structures of (3+1)-dimensional equations to find non-traveling lumps and breathers is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in the specific functional decoupling within the Hirota formalism tailored for high-dimensional systems, moving beyond the ubiquitous z = n ± ct assumption.

proposals.title: Soliton and Lump Solutions for Integrable Differential-Difference Equations with Spatially and Temporally Varying Coefficients
proposals.motivation: Integrable differential-difference equations (DDEs) with constant coefficients are well-studied, but real-world applications in lattice dynamics, nonlinear optics, and biology often involve systems with inhomogeneities or external forces, modeled by variable coefficients. Pre-2026, the extension of powerful solution methods like the Hirota bilinear method and symbolic computation to such non-autonomous DDEs remains a significant challenge, limiting the modeling of more complex physical scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: That the established frameworks for constructing multiple soliton, lump, and traveling wave solutions for constant-coefficient integrable DDEs can be systematically adapted and generalized to handle coefficients that are explicit functions of the discrete index n and continuous time t, leading to new, physically relevant solution structures.
proposals.method: We will focus on a canonical, integrable DDE, such as a variable-coefficient Toda lattice or Ablowitz-Ladik model. The core approach is to first derive a generalized bilinear form using a suitable variable-dependent transformation. We will then apply an extended ansatz for the tau-function within the Hirota formalism, where the phase shifts and wave numbers are promoted to functions of n and t. Symbolic computation (e.g., in Maple or Mathematica) will be used to solve the resulting constraint equations determining these functions and the allowable forms of the variable coefficients for integrability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new class of exact solutions for non-autonomous integrable DDEs, explicitly demonstrating how solitons and lumps interact with a variable background. It will provide a methodological template for extending other solution techniques (e.g., the exp-function method) to variable-coefficient discrete systems, thereby enhancing the toolbox for applied mathematicians and theoretical physicists.
proposals.novelty: While variable-coefficient PDEs have been studied, the systematic adaptation of the lump solution method and the detailed analysis of dynamical wave-form patterns for DDEs with dual dependence on n and t is a novel and non-trivial extension of pre-2026 techniques, moving beyond the common focus on solely time-dependent coefficients.

proposals.title: Painlevé Integrability and Soliton Solutions for a Novel (2+1)-Dimensional Differential-Difference Equation
proposals.motivation: The Painlevé test is a powerful, algorithmic tool for identifying integrable continuous nonlinear PDEs, leading to the discovery of rich solution structures like solitons. However, its systematic adaptation to discrete or differential-difference equations (DDEs) remains less developed, creating a gap in the rigorous classification of integrable lattice systems. This project aims to bridge this gap, providing a concrete framework to test DDEs for integrability, which is crucial for modeling nonlinear wave phenomena in discrete media.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a rigorous, singularity-structure-based Painlevé analysis can be successfully formulated for a specific class of (2+1)-dimensional DDEs, and that passing this test will guarantee the existence of multiple soliton and lump-type solutions obtainable via Hirota's bilinear method.
proposals.method: We will first define a novel (2+1)-dimensional DDE as a candidate system, extending known integrable hierarchies. The core technical work involves adapting the Ablowitz-Ramani-Segur (ARS) algorithm to the discrete domain: we will seek a formal series solution of the DDE, u_n(x,y,t) = φ(n,x,y,t)^(-α) Σ_{j=0}^∞ u_j(n,x,y,t) φ(n,x,y,t)^j, and determine the leading-order exponent α and resonances. The requirement that the series be a full Laurent expansion with the correct number of arbitrary functions at each resonance will constitute the integrability condition. Successful analysis will be followed by constructing soliton solutions using the discrete Hirota bilinearization technique.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a concrete, testable Painlevé integrability criterion for a new (2+1)-dimensional DDE, significantly extending the toolkit for studying discrete integrable systems. It will yield new, exact multi-soliton, lump, and traveling wave solutions for the proposed equation, enriching the catalog of analytically tractable models for nonlinear wave dynamics in higher-dimensional lattices.
proposals.novelty: While Painlevé analysis for (1+1)-dimensional DDEs exists, its application to systematically discover and validate new (2+1)-dimensional differential-difference equations is novel. The combination of a rigorous discrete Painlevé test with the subsequent derivation of diverse solution types (solitons, lumps) for the same equation represents a significant, non-trivial advancement beyond standard solution techniques applied to pre-existing equations.

proposals.title: Lie Symmetry Analysis and Soliton Solutions for a Novel Integrable (2+1)-Dimensional Differential-Difference Equation
proposals.motivation: Differential-difference equations (DDEs) are fundamental models for spatially discrete or lattice systems in physics and biology. While symmetry methods are powerful for continuous PDEs, their systematic application to DDEs, especially in higher dimensions, remains less developed. Extending these methods to (2+1)-dimensional DDEs can unlock new integrable models and solution structures beyond standard semi-discrete soliton equations.
proposals.hypothesis: That a systematic Lie symmetry approach, adapted for DDEs, can be applied to derive symmetry reductions, conservation laws, and novel soliton-like solutions for a newly proposed (2+1)-dimensional integrable DDE, generalizing the continuous results familiar from Painlevé analysis.
proposals.method: We will first propose a new (2+1)-dimensional DDE, ensuring its integrability via the discrete Painlevé test. The core method is the application of Lie group analysis to DDEs: we will calculate the Lie point symmetries by considering the equation and its shifted copies as a continuous system. This will yield the symmetry generators and corresponding group-invariant solutions. We will then perform symmetry reductions to obtain ordinary difference equations. Solutions will be sought using ansätze for traveling waves, rational functions (for lump-type structures), and the Hirota bilinear method (for multi-soliton solutions) adapted to the discrete setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, integrable (2+1)-dimensional DDE model for lattice dynamics. It will deliver a complete Lie symmetry classification, explicit symmetry reductions, and a variety of exact solutions including solitons, lumps, and traveling waves. The project will demonstrate a concrete methodology for applying symmetry techniques to higher-dimensional discrete systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of Lie symmetry analysis, typically for PDEs, with the study of (2+1)-dimensional DDEs, moving beyond the more common (1+1)-dimensional semi-discrete or purely continuous (3+1)-dimensional models prevalent in the researcher's recent work. The focus on deriving lump and interaction solutions directly from symmetry principles in a discrete spatial setting is also new.

proposals.title: Analytical Approximations for Nonlinear Delay Differential Equations via the Homotopy Analysis Method: Application to Soliton Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Delay differential equations (DDEs) are crucial for modeling phenomena with memory or time lags in physics and biology, but they are notoriously difficult to solve analytically. While powerful for PDEs, the homotopy analysis method (HAM) has seen limited systematic application to complex, nonlinear DDEs, particularly those arising in integrable systems theory where exact soliton solutions for PDEs are known. Bridging this methodological gap is essential for obtaining analytical insights into delayed wave dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: The homotopy analysis method, with an optimally chosen auxiliary linear operator and convergence-control parameter, can generate accurate analytical approximations for physically significant DDEs that lack closed-form exact solutions, effectively capturing delayed soliton-type behaviors.
proposals.method: We will adapt the HAM framework to handle DDEs by constructing a homotopy that embeds the delay term. The method will be applied to specific, high-order nonlinear DDEs derived as semi-discrete reductions or analogues of known integrable (3+1)-dimensional systems studied by Wazwaz (e.g., related to the pKP–BKP hierarchy). The auxiliary linear operator will be chosen based on the dominant linear part of the DDE, and the convergence-control parameter ℏ will be optimized via the squared residual error. Solutions will be sought in the form of series approximations for traveling wave profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust analytical toolkit for approximating solutions to a challenging class of DDEs, extending the utility of HAM beyond ODEs and PDEs. It will yield new, verifiable approximate solutions for delayed wave models, contributing to the understanding of how time lags affect soliton propagation and interaction patterns in integrable-like settings.
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis applies HAM's powerful approximation scheme specifically to DDEs linked to high-dimensional, Painlevé-integrable soliton equations, a combination not deeply explored in pre-2026 literature. It moves beyond standard DDE solution techniques like perturbation or numerical methods to obtain parameter-tunable analytical series.

proposals.title: Generalized Bilinearization and Soliton Solutions for Regular Higher-Dimensional Integrable Equations
proposals.motivation: The Painlevé integrability approach and associated ansatz methods (like the simplified Hirota bilinear method) have been highly successful for constructing exact solutions to singular or weakly singular nonlinear evolution equations. However, their systematic application to regular boundary value problems and equations with smooth, non-singular coefficients remains underexplored. Extending these powerful soliton-solving techniques to this broader, physically prevalent class of problems would significantly expand the toolkit for analytical studies in nonlinear wave dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: The core integrability ansatzes and bilinearization procedures developed for singular integrable equations can be systematically adapted and generalized to construct novel lump, soliton, and traveling wave solutions for regular (non-singular) higher-dimensional integrable systems with smooth coefficients.
proposals.method: We will select a canonical regular (3+1)-dimensional integrable equation, such as a regularized version of the KP or BKP-type equations with smooth polynomial coefficients. The approach will adapt the simplified Hirota bilinear method: first, we will apply a suitable dependent variable transformation to convert the nonlinear PDE into a bilinear form. We will then employ generalized exponential, rational, and mixed ansatz functions within the Hirota D-operator formalism to derive N-soliton solutions, periodic waves, and lump solutions. The analysis will specifically focus on managing the regular structure during the bilinearization and solution construction phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, systematic framework for generating exact solutions to a wider class of regular integrable PDEs, directly applicable to wave phenomena in continuous media with smooth properties. It will yield new families of explicit soliton and lump solutions, enriching the catalog of known analytical solutions and offering deeper insight into the wave interaction dynamics within these regular systems.
proposals.novelty: While bilinear methods are standard for singular integrable equations, their deliberate adaptation and demonstration for regular boundary value problems with non-singular coefficients is a non-trivial and novel extension, moving beyond the typical domain of Painlevé-integrable singular models.

proposals.title: Lump, Soliton, and Traveling Wave Solutions for (3+1)-Dimensional Integrable Equations with Exponential and Trigonometric Nonlinearities
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, the application of powerful analytical techniques like the Hirota bilinear method, simplified Hirota's method, and ansatz-based approaches has been predominantly focused on integrable equations with polynomial nonlinearities. Many physically significant models, such as those in nonlinear optics, fluid dynamics, and quantum field theory, feature non-polynomial nonlinearities like sine-Gordon or sinh-Gordon types. Testing the robustness and adaptability of these established soliton solution methods on such equations remains an underexplored area of nonlinear science.
proposals.hypothesis: The systematic application of the simplified Hirota's method, combined with specific ansatz functions for lump and traveling waves, can be successfully extended to construct novel exact solutions—including multi-solitons, lump solutions, and periodic waves—for (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations possessing exponential or trigonometric nonlinear terms.
proposals.method: We will select a specific (3+1)-dimensional integrable equation with an exponential nonlinearity, such as a (3+1)D sinh-Gordon-type equation derived via the Painlevé analysis. The core technique will be the application of the simplified Hirota's method, which requires transforming the equation into a bilinear form using a logarithmic transformation tailored for the specific nonlinearity. For lump solutions, a quadratic function ansatz will be substituted into the established bilinear form. For traveling wave solutions, a direct ansatz method will be employed, and the consistency will be verified through symbolic computation software like Maple or Mathematica.
proposals.contribution: This work will significantly extend the known solution repertoire for a new class of (3+1)-dimensional integrable systems, providing concrete families of lump, soliton, and traveling wave solutions. It will demonstrate the versatility of pre-2026 analytical techniques, offering a methodological blueprint for handling non-polynomial nonlinearities in higher-dimensional settings and enriching the understanding of wave interaction dynamics in such models.
proposals.novelty: While the researcher has extensively studied polynomial nonlinear (3+1)D equations, the targeted application of these solution techniques to (3+1)-dimensional integrable models with explicit exponential/trigonometric nonlinearities is a novel and non-trivial extension, moving beyond the well-trodden domain of polynomial-type equations like KdV, KP, or BKP variants.

proposals.title: Analytical Solutions for Coupled Multi-Physics Boundary Value Problems via a Modified Adomian Decomposition Method
proposals.motivation: Many complex phenomena in fluid-structure interaction, thermoelasticity, and plasma physics are modeled by systems of coupled nonlinear ODEs with boundary conditions. Pre-2026, analytical methods for such coupled boundary value problems (BVPs) are limited, often relying on restrictive linearizations or purely numerical approaches, which obscure the functional structure of solutions. Extending robust analytical techniques to these systems is crucial for gaining deeper physical insight and generating benchmark solutions.
proposals.hypothesis: The Modified Adomian Decomposition Method (MADM), previously effective for single high-order and (3+1)-dimensional PDEs, can be systematically adapted to generate convergent series solutions for strongly coupled, nonlinear ODE systems subject to multi-point boundary conditions, preserving integrability structures where they exist.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithmic framework extending the MADM to coupled ODE systems. The core technical steps are: (1) Reformulating the coupled BVPs into a system of coupled integral equations using appropriate Green's functions to incorporate boundary conditions directly into the recursive scheme. (2) Generating coupled Adomian polynomials for the nonlinear terms in each equation using a modified algorithm (e.g., the Duan-Rach approach) to handle cross-coupling terms efficiently. (3) Implementing a recursive solution algorithm to compute the series components, with convergence acceleration techniques applied. The method will be tested on canonical coupled systems derived from reductions of integrable (3+1)-D equations studied by the researcher.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new analytical toolset for solving coupled multi-physics BVPs, generating explicit series solutions that reveal parametric dependencies. It will extend the applicability of decomposition methods from single equations to interactive systems, offering an alternative to purely numerical solvers for certain classes of problems and enriching the study of solution structures in applied mathematics.
proposals.novelty: While the MADM is established for single equations, its systematic extension to *coupled* nonlinear ODEs with multi-point boundary conditions, particularly those derived from integrable systems, is a non-trivial and underexplored advancement. The integration of boundary conditions directly into the coupled recursive algorithm distinguishes it from standard shooting or weighting methods.

proposals.title: Inverse Parameter Identification for (3+1)-Dimensional Integrable Equations via Bilinearization and Optimization
proposals.motivation: In nonlinear wave physics and engineering, governing equations often contain unknown parameters that must be estimated from observed data, such as wave profiles or soliton interactions. While forward solvers for constructing solutions to integrable (3+1)-dimensional equations (e.g., pKP–BKP, Estevez-Prada) are well-developed, the corresponding inverse problem of parameter identification remains a significant challenge. Efficiently determining these parameters is crucial for model validation and predictive capability in fields like fluid dynamics and nonlinear optics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by coupling the established Hirota bilinear method for generating exact forward solutions (multiple solitons, lumps, traveling waves) with a gradient-based optimization framework, unknown constant parameters in the nonlinear terms of (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations can be accurately and robustly identified from synthetic or experimental spatio-temporal waveform data.
proposals.method: The method will first employ the Hirota bilinear formalism to derive analytic forward solvers for a target equation with symbolic parameters. This solver will be integrated into an objective function measuring the discrepancy between predicted and observed wave patterns. We will implement a Levenberg-Marquardt or adjoint-based optimization algorithm to minimize this function, iteratively adjusting the symbolic parameters. The approach will be tested on the combined pKP–BKP and the Painlevé integrable Estevez-Prada equations, using their known soliton and lump solutions as synthetic 'data' with added noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational pipeline for parameter estimation in high-dimensional nonlinear integrable systems, directly extending the researcher's expertise in forward solution construction. It will deliver specific identified parameter sets for model equations and a benchmark for the robustness of the method against data noise and incomplete observations, bridging the gap between exact solution theory and practical inverse problems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted application of bilinear-based forward solvers—a staple in Dr. Wazwaz's recent work—specifically to the inverse parameter problem for (3+1)-dimensional equations, a task typically addressed for lower-dimensional systems or via purely numerical PDE solvers without exploiting integrable structure.

proposals.title: A PECAS Framework for Deriving Soliton and Traveling Wave Solutions to Systems of Coupled Integrable PDEs
proposals.motivation: The study of integrable systems of coupled partial differential equations (PDEs) is central to modeling complex nonlinear phenomena in physics and engineering, from nonlinear optics to fluid dynamics. While the Painlevé Expansion, Consistent-Anstaz, and Simplified Hirota (PECAS) framework has proven effective for single, high-dimensional equations, its systematic application to coupled systems remains underdeveloped. Extending this framework is crucial for generating and classifying novel multi-component soliton and interaction solutions in a unified, algorithmic manner.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the PECAS framework can be systematically generalized to handle systems of coupled, integrable PDEs, enabling the automated derivation of multi-soliton, lump, and traveling wave solutions that capture the intricate dynamical interactions between different field components.
proposals.method: We will first formalize the extension of the Painlevé test to multi-component systems to identify integrable couplings. The simplified Hirota bilinear method will be adapted by introducing auxiliary functions for each field component to derive N-soliton solutions. For systems admitting lump or traveling wave solutions, we will develop a coupled consistent ansatz approach, where the ansatz for each dependent variable is interlinked via the system's constraints. The methodology will be concretely demonstrated on coupled (3+1)-dimensional systems, such as extensions of the KP and BKP hierarchies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, algorithmic toolkit for solving systems of coupled integrable PDEs, significantly expanding the solution space available to researchers. It will yield new, explicit multi-component soliton and interaction solutions, deepening the understanding of wave dynamics in complex, multi-field physical systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, integrated application of the PECAS framework specifically to *coupled* systems, moving beyond its well-established use for single equations, and in developing the interlinked ansatz structures required for their consistent solution.

proposals.title: Generalized Soliton and Lump Solutions for (3+1)-Dimensional Equations with Non-Integer Singular Coefficients
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, the study of integrable equations, such as the pKP–BKP and Estevez-Prada models, has largely focused on constant or integer-order singular coefficients. However, many physical phenomena in nonlinear optics, plasma physics, and fluid dynamics are modeled by equations with variable or fractional-order coefficients, which introduce significant analytical challenges. Extending integrability methods to such non-standard equations is crucial for broadening the applicability of soliton theory to more realistic, complex systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That the Hirota bilinear method and symbolic computation techniques can be systematically generalized to construct exact soliton, lump, and traveling wave solutions for (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations featuring non-integer (e.g., fractional) or spatially/temporally variable singular coefficients.
proposals.method: We will first adapt the Painlevé integrability test to accommodate equations with non-integer singular coefficients, identifying constraints for integrability. The core approach will be a modified Hirota bilinearization, where the dependent variable transformation is extended to incorporate the variable or fractional coefficient structure. Using symbolic computation in *Mathematica* or *Maple*, we will derive generalized N-soliton solutions and employ ansatz methods for lump and rogue wave solutions. The stability and interaction dynamics of the obtained solutions will be analyzed via graphical simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new class of exact solutions for previously intractable nonlinear evolution equations, directly extending the researcher's recent work on (3+1)-dimensional models. It will provide a methodological framework for handling non-integer singularities, enriching the toolbox for studying non-standard integrable systems. The results will be validated through dynamical analysis of the novel wave patterns.
proposals.novelty: While lump and soliton solutions for constant-coefficient equations are well-established, the explicit construction of such solutions for (3+1)-dimensional equations with *non-integer* or *variable* singular coefficients is largely unexplored. The proposed adaptation of the Hirota method to this context represents a significant technical advancement beyond pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Soliton and Traveling Wave Solutions for a Time-Dependent Parabolic Lane-Emden-Type Equation: A Painlevé Integrability and Bilinear Approach
proposals.motivation: The classical Lane-Emden equation is a cornerstone for modeling stellar structure and isotropic astrophysical objects, but its static formulation limits its application to dynamic phenomena like collapsing gas clouds or time-evolving stellar atmospheres. A time-dependent, parabolic generalization creates a more realistic (3+1)-dimensional model for such systems, yet its integrability properties and localized wave solutions remain largely unexplored in pre-2026 literature, presenting a significant gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a suitably defined time-dependent parabolic Lane-Emden-type equation can pass the Painlevé integrability test, allowing for the construction of multiple soliton, lump, and diverse traveling wave solutions using advanced bilinear and symbolic computation techniques.
proposals.method: We will first propose a novel (3+1)-dimensional parabolic Lane-Emden model incorporating a time-derivative term. The Painlevé analysis will be systematically applied to establish its integrability. For the integrable case, we will employ Hirota's bilinear method, extended to higher dimensions as used in Wazwaz's recent work, to derive multi-soliton solutions. Additionally, we will utilize symbolic computation (e.g., Mathematica) with ansatz-based methods (e.g., the positive quadratic function method for lumps, tanh/coth methods) to extract a wider variety of solutions, including periodic, kink, and rogue-wave-type structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield the first systematic study of an integrable, time-dependent Lane-Emden model, providing a new analytical framework for dynamic astrophysical and fluid-dynamic systems. It will contribute a novel family of exact solutions—solitons, lumps, and traveling waves—to the nonlinear wave theory literature, extending the applicability of integrable systems techniques to a new class of physically significant equations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the astrophysically motivated Lane-Emden structure with the formalism of (3+1)-dimensional integrable systems and soliton theory, a synthesis not previously attempted, and in applying Painlevé analysis and bilinear methods specifically to this parabolic generalization.

proposals.title: Beyond Simplified Forms: A Full Bilinear Approach to (3+1)-Dimensional Integrable Equations for Novel Solution Structures
proposals.motivation: The bilinear Hirota method is a cornerstone for constructing soliton and lump solutions in integrable systems. However, to achieve tractability, a common practice is to impose simplifying constraints on the auxiliary functions or the bilinear form itself after the dependent variable transformation. This simplification, while effective for deriving standard multi-soliton solutions, may artificially restrict the solution space, potentially obscuring richer structures like resonant solitons, complex interaction patterns, or exotic localized waves that satisfy the full, unconstrained bilinear equations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically applying the full, non-simplified bilinear formalism to higher-dimensional integrable equations, without imposing ad-hoc constraints, will reveal previously unreported classes of exact solutions and more general interaction dynamics that are lost in the simplified approach.
proposals.method: We will select a prototypical (3+1)-dimensional integrable equation from the Painlevé class, such as the combined pKP-BKP or the Estevez-Prada equation studied by Wazwaz. The standard logarithmic transformation will be applied to obtain its exact bilinear form. Instead of simplifying the auxiliary function ansatz (e.g., forcing a standard polynomial-exponential form), we will solve the resulting system of bilinear equations in its entirety using a generalized, computer-algebra-assisted ansatz. This involves employing a more flexible test function, potentially combining exponential, polynomial, and trigonometric terms with independent coefficients for the (3+1) variables, and solving the full set of determining equations generated by the Hirota D-operators.
proposals.contribution: This work will rigorously test the limits of the simplified bilinear method and map the complete solution subspace accessible via the full formalism. It is expected to yield new families of solutions—such as hybrid lump-soliton structures, degenerate solitons with novel interactions, or quasi-periodic waves—for key (3+1)-dimensional models. This will provide a more comprehensive understanding of solution diversity in integrable systems and offer a revised methodological framework for future studies.
proposals.novelty: While the bilinear method is well-established, its application is almost universally followed by simplification for convenience. This proposal deliberately avoids this step, treating the full bilinear system as the primary object of study, which is a significant and non-routine deviation from standard practice in the pre-2026 literature on soliton theory.

proposals.title: Solitons and Rogue Waves in (3+1)-Dimensional Integrable Systems with Non-Vanishing Boundary Conditions
proposals.motivation: The analysis of integrable (3+1)-dimensional equations, such as the combined pKP–BKP and Estevez-Prada equations, has predominantly assumed vanishing boundary conditions (VBCs) for mathematical tractability. However, realistic physical contexts—like deep-water wave backgrounds, optical media with constant background intensity, or plasma with a non-zero ambient potential—require non-vanishing boundary conditions (NVBCs). This gap limits the applicability of existing soliton and lump solutions to real-world phenomena, including the generation of rogue waves on a non-zero background.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically imposing non-vanishing or periodic boundary conditions on key (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations, we can derive novel classes of localized solutions—including dark solitons, breathers, and rogue waves—that exhibit richer interaction dynamics and more physically relevant structures than their VBC counterparts.
proposals.method: We will employ the Hirota bilinear method, adapted for NVBCs by introducing a suitable background transformation (e.g., u = u_0 + f(φ) where u_0 is the constant background). For periodic boundary conditions, we will explore the use of Jacobi elliptic functions as seed solutions within the simplified Hirota formalism. The Painlevé integrability test will first confirm the chosen equations remain integrable under these constraints. Subsequently, we will construct N-soliton solutions via perturbation expansions in the bilinear form and use symbolic computation (Maple/Mathematica) to analyze their asymptotic behavior and interaction patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the known solution spaces of several important (3+1)-dimensional integrable systems to physically relevant backgrounds, providing explicit analytical formulas for new wave structures. It will establish a methodological framework for handling NVBCs in high-dimensional integrable PDEs, directly contributing to nonlinear wave theory and its applications in fluid dynamics and nonlinear optics.
proposals.novelty: While NVBCs have been studied for (1+1)- and (2+1)-dimensional systems, their consistent application and the derivation of diverse solution types for the complex, recently discovered (3+1)-dimensional Painlevé-integrable equations represent a significant and unexplored advancement. The focus on periodic boundaries for these equations is particularly novel.

proposals.title: Lump, Soliton, and Interaction Solutions for a (3+1)-Dimensional Variable-Coefficient CDG-Type Equation
proposals.motivation: Constant-coefficient integrable equations, while foundational, often fail to model real-world phenomena in inhomogeneous or non-uniform media, such as optical fibers with varying dispersion or plasma with density gradients. Extending integrable models like the Caudrey-Dodd-Gibbon (CDG) or combined pKP-BKP equations to include variable coefficients is a crucial step toward physical realism. Pre-2026 research by Wazwaz and others has successfully explored exact solutions for constant-coefficient (3+1)-D equations, but the variable-coefficient case remains less charted, especially for complex localized structures.
proposals.hypothesis: That a suitably constrained (3+1)-dimensional variable-coefficient CDG-type equation retains sufficient integrability to allow for the construction of novel lump waves, multi-solitons, and their interaction solutions via generalized ansatz and symbolic computation methods.
proposals.method: We will first formulate a (3+1)-D combined pKP-BKP-type equation with spatially/temporally dependent coefficients. The Painlevé integrability test will be applied to derive constraints on the variable coefficients. Using Hirota's bilinear method extended to variable-coefficient cases, we will construct N-soliton solutions. For lump and rogue-wave-type solutions, we will employ a positive quadratic function ansatz within the bilinear framework. Symbolic computation (Maple/Mathematica) will be essential for solving the resulting determining equations for the variable coefficients and solution parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, physically more relevant integrable model and provide a library of exact solutions (lumps, solitons, breathers, interactions) showcasing how variable coefficients alter wave dynamics. It will extend the established solution techniques of the researcher to a more complex and applicable class of equations, contributing to the understanding of wave propagation in non-homogeneous media.
proposals.novelty: While variable-coefficient KdV and NLS equations have been studied, the application of lump solution and multi-soliton interaction techniques to a (3+1)-dimensional variable-coefficient CDG-type integrable system is novel. The explicit demonstration of how spatial/temporal inhomogeneity controls soliton fission, fusion, and lump stability will be a new result.

proposals.title: Complete Integrability Analysis of the (3+1)-Dimensional CDG and CDG-KP Equations: Lax Pairs, Conservation Laws, and Hamiltonian Structures
proposals.motivation: The (3+1)-dimensional Caudrey-Dodd-Gibbon (CDG) and CDG-Kadomtsev-Petviashvili (CDG-KP) equations are significant extensions of classical integrable models, capable of describing complex nonlinear wave phenomena in higher dimensions. While recent studies, including those by Wazwaz, have successfully constructed various soliton and traveling wave solutions, a systematic investigation of their fundamental integrability properties remains incomplete. Establishing these properties—Lax pairs, infinite conservation laws, and Hamiltonian structures—is crucial for confirming their complete integrability and enabling advanced analytical techniques like inverse scattering and symmetry analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the (3+1)-dimensional CDG and CDG-KP equations possess non-trivial Lax representations, an infinite hierarchy of conservation laws, and a bi-Hamiltonian structure, thereby confirming their status as completely integrable systems in the sense of Lax.
proposals.method: The research will employ established symbolic computation and ansatz-based methods grounded in pre-2026 integrable systems theory. First, we will apply the Painlevé analysis to test for integrability and guide the construction of Lax pairs using a simplified bilinear approach and a suitable ansatz for the Lax operators. Second, we will derive an infinite sequence of conservation laws via the Lax pair and the recursive operator method. Finally, we will utilize the trace identity or the variational derivative approach to identify compatible Hamiltonian operators and construct the corresponding Hamiltonian functionals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first complete integrability framework for the (3+1)-dimensional CDG and CDG-KP equations, formally placing them within the hierarchy of known integrable systems. The explicit Lax pairs and Hamiltonian structures will unlock new methods for generating solutions and studying their long-time dynamics, while the conservation laws will offer physical insights into the systems' conserved quantities.
proposals.novelty: While solution structures for these equations have been explored, a dedicated, unified study to establish their Lax integrability and Hamiltonian formulation in (3+1) dimensions is novel and non-trivial, moving beyond solution construction to foundational mathematical structure.

proposals.title: Painlevé Integrability Analysis of the (3+1)-Dimensional Caudrey-Dodd-Gibbon and Caudrey-Dodd-Gibbon-Kadomtsev-Petviashvili Equations
proposals.motivation: The (3+1)-dimensional Caudrey-Dodd-Gibbon (CDG) and its KP-type extension (CDG-KP) are high-order nonlinear evolution equations of significant interest in soliton theory and fluid dynamics. Prior to 2026, their integrability has often been assumed based on the existence of soliton solutions derived through ansatz methods like Hirota's bilinear form. A rigorous, independent verification using the Painlevé test is essential to confirm their complete integrability and to understand the structure of their general solutions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that both the (3+1)-dimensional CDG and CDG-KP equations will pass the Painlevé test for integrability in the WTC-Kruskal sense, confirming their status as Painlevé-integrable systems and guaranteeing the existence of general solutions without movable critical singularities.
proposals.method: We will systematically apply the Weiss-Tabor-Carnevale (WTC) algorithm with the Kruskal simplification. First, we determine the leading-order behavior and resonances for each equation by substituting the Laurent series expansion u(x,y,z,t) = φ^(α) Σ_{j=0}^∞ u_j φ^j, where φ is the singularity manifold. We will then verify the compatibility conditions at each resonance level. The analysis will be performed using symbolic computation software (e.g., Maple or Mathematica) to handle the complex algebra inherent in these (3+1)-dimensional systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, foundational proof of integrability for these important (3+1)-dimensional models, moving beyond solution construction to a fundamental property test. It will establish a rigorous basis for future studies on their Lax pairs, Bäcklund transformations, and other integrable features. The results will also clarify the relationship between the CDG and CDG-KP hierarchies in higher dimensions.
proposals.novelty: While soliton solutions for these equations have been studied, a dedicated and detailed Painlevé analysis for the full (3+1)-dimensional CDG and CDG-KP equations, to confirm their integrability from first principles, has not been comprehensively reported in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Numerical Validation of Soliton and Lump Solutions in (3+1)-Dimensional Integrable Equations via Fourier Spectral Methods
proposals.motivation: The derivation of novel soliton, lump, and traveling wave solutions for complex (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations (e.g., pKP-BKP, Estevez-Prada) is a core activity in nonlinear science, but analytical solutions often lack rigorous numerical verification. Prior to 2026, the validation of such multi-dimensional solutions typically relied on simplified ansatzes or lower-dimensional reductions, leaving a gap in confirming their dynamical stability and interaction properties under full spatial evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the exact analytical soliton and wave solutions derived for recently studied (3+1)-dimensional Painlevé-integrable equations will be validated with high precision by direct numerical simulation, confirming their stability and interaction dynamics as true solutions of the full PDE system.
proposals.method: We will implement a high-accuracy numerical scheme based on Fourier pseudo-spectral methods for spatial discretization, leveraging the FFTW library for efficiency. A fourth-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) scheme will handle time integration. The numerical domain will be designed with periodic boundary conditions and sufficient padding to mitigate aliasing, using an integrating factor technique to handle stiff linear terms. The initial condition will be set using the exact analytical soliton solution, and its evolution will be compared quantitatively to the theoretical prediction via L2-norm error analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust numerical benchmark for the analytical solutions produced by the researcher, strengthening confidence in their correctness and physical relevance. It will also demonstrate a reproducible spectral framework for testing future solutions in (3+1)-dimensional integrable systems, bridging a gap between symbolic computation and computational PDE analysis.
proposals.novelty: While spectral methods are established, their systematic application for the direct validation of *specific* newly derived lump and multi-soliton solutions in full (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations, as opposed to model equations or (1+1)-D cases, represents a novel and necessary step in the solution verification pipeline for this research domain.

proposals.title: Dimensional Reduction and Soliton Dynamics: From the (3+1)-Dimensional CDG-KP Equation to the (2+1)-Dimensional CDG System
proposals.motivation: The (3+1)-dimensional Caudrey-Dodd-Gibbon-Kadomtsev-Petviashvili (CDG-KP) equation is a significant integrable model that generalizes the KdV hierarchy and describes nonlinear wave phenomena in complex media. Understanding its connection to lower-dimensional integrable systems through symmetry constraints is a fundamental problem in soliton theory. A systematic study of solutions independent of one transverse variable, which reduces CDG-KP to the well-known (2+1)-dimensional CDG (or KP equation with a self-consistent source), remains underexplored and is crucial for classifying solution hierarchies and understanding dimensional transitions in integrable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that imposing a translational symmetry along one transverse coordinate on the CDG-KP equation will yield a consistent dimensional reduction to the CDG system, and that the rich solution structure of CDG-KP (including lumps, breathers, and interaction solutions) will project onto novel, non-trivial solution families for the reduced CDG equation, preserving integrability properties.
proposals.method: We will employ the Hirota bilinear formalism to construct the N-soliton solution for the CDG-KP equation. The dimensional reduction condition (independence from the variable 'y') will be implemented as a constraint on the wave vectors and phases in the bilinear ansatz. This will generate explicit constraints linking solution parameters, effectively reducing the bilinear form. We will then analyze the resulting solutions using symbolic computation (Maple/Mathematica) to derive lump solutions via long-wave limits, periodic wave solutions, and investigate resonant and fusion/fission phenomena induced by the constraint. The Painlevé integrability test will be applied to the reduced equation to confirm its integrable character.
proposals.contribution: This work will explicitly map the solution space of the (3+1)D CDG-KP equation to that of the (2+1)D CDG system, providing a new class of exact solutions for the latter. It will clarify the role of symmetry reductions in generating complex wave patterns from higher-dimensional integrable models. The results will contribute a systematic case study on dimensional reduction within the KP hierarchy, of interest in mathematical physics and nonlinear wave theory.
proposals.novelty: While the CDG-KP and CDG equations are individually studied, the explicit, solution-based connection via a symmetry constraint, and the subsequent derivation of lump and interaction solutions for the CDG system directly from the CDG-KP reduction, constitutes a novel and non-trivial synthesis. This approach moves beyond a simple formal reduction to a concrete exploration of the inherited solution dynamics.

proposals.title: Inverse Operator Identification via Adomian Decomposition: Reconstructing Nonlinear PDEs from Known Soliton and Lump Solutions
proposals.motivation: The direct problem of finding solutions for given nonlinear partial differential equations (PDEs) is well-studied, but the inverse problem—determining the governing equation from known solution structures—remains a significant challenge. This is crucial for modeling complex phenomena where the underlying physics is not fully specified but key wave behaviors, like solitons and lumps, are observed. Pre-2026, methods for systematic operator identification from multi-soliton solutions are underdeveloped, limiting data-driven model discovery in nonlinear wave theory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Adomian polynomial decomposition of a nonlinear operator, when applied inversely to a known exact solution (or a superposition of solutions), yields a deterministic system of algebraic equations from which the operator's explicit form can be uniquely reconstructed.
proposals.method: The method begins by assuming a general polynomial ansatz for a (3+1)-dimensional nonlinear integrable operator. Known multi-soliton, lump, or traveling wave solutions from Wazwaz's recent work are substituted into this ansatz. The nonlinear terms are decomposed using Adomian polynomials, which treat the unknown operator coefficients as parameters. This transforms the PDE into an infinite series where coefficients of like terms must vanish identically. Truncating the series at an appropriate order generates a finite, over-determined linear system for the operator's coefficients, solvable via symbolic computation. The approach will be validated on known integrable equations like the combined pKP-BKP before being applied to infer operators from novel solution families.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, systematic algorithm for the inverse scattering transform's conceptual counterpart: deriving equations from solutions. It will extend the utility of Adomian decomposition beyond solution-finding to model discovery. The results will offer a new tool for verifying the integrability of proposed equations and for constructing new candidate models that possess prescribed solution properties.
proposals.novelty: While Adomian polynomials are standard for forward solution computation, their inversion to identify an unknown differential operator is a novel application. This approach fundamentally reorients the methodology from solving a given equation to engineering an equation that reproduces observed coherent structures.

proposals.title: Algorithmic Extension for Oscillatory Soliton Solutions in (3+1)-Dimensional Nonlinear PDEs: Application to a Generalized Painlevé-Integrable System
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, the study of (3+1)-dimensional nonlinear partial differential equations (PDEs) is crucial for modeling complex wave phenomena in physics and engineering. While powerful symbolic algorithms exist for constructing multiple soliton and lump solutions, their direct application to oscillatory models—characterized by complex wave structures and interactions—remains underexplored. Extending these computational methods is essential for systematically uncovering new, physically relevant wave patterns in high-dimensional integrable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the symbolic algorithm for constructing multiple soliton solutions can be systematically generalized and implemented to generate novel oscillatory and hybrid (soliton-lump-periodic) wave solutions for a new, broadly defined (3+1)-dimensional Painlevé-integrable equation. This will reveal previously unreported dynamical interaction patterns.
proposals.method: We will first define a generalized (3+1)-dimensional fifth-order PDE that passes the Painlevé integrability test, ensuring a rich solution structure. The core method involves extending the Hirota bilinear formalism and the associated symbolic algorithm to incorporate complex phase shifts and periodic functions. Specifically, we will implement a modified version of the Hereman-Nuseir algorithm in symbolic software (like Maple or Mathematica) to handle ansätze combining exponential (soliton), rational (lump), and trigonometric (periodic) functions. The algorithm will be tested for consistency and used to derive new solution classes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, computationally efficient algorithm tailored for oscillatory nonlinear PDEs, generating a novel catalog of exact solutions (including breathers, oscillatory lumps, and soliton-periodic hybrids) for the proposed model. It will enhance the toolkit for analyzing complex wave dynamics in integrable systems and provide a template for extending similar algorithms to other high-dimensional equations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted algorithmic extension to handle oscillatory components within the established soliton/lump framework for (3+1)-D PDEs, moving beyond standard multi-soliton solutions. The proposed generalized equation and the hybrid solution structures it yields are not trivially obtainable from pre-2026 studies on specific models like the pKP-BKP or Estevez-Prada equations.

proposals.title: Lump, Soliton, and Traveling Wave Solutions for a Class of (3+1)-Dimensional Nonlinear Delay Differential Equations
proposals.motivation: Nonlinear delay differential equations (DDEs) are crucial for modeling real-world phenomena with memory and propagation lags, such as in biological systems, neural networks, and control theory. However, pre-2026 analytical techniques for obtaining exact solutions, like solitons and lumps, have been predominantly applied to standard PDEs without explicit time delays. Extending these powerful integrability-based methods to DDEs remains a significant, underexplored challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: That the polynomial calculation algorithm, recently developed for constructing multiple soliton solutions to integrable (3+1)-dimensional PDEs, can be successfully adapted and applied to a specific class of nonlinear, high-dimensional DDEs to derive novel exact solutions, including delayed lumps and interaction patterns.
proposals.method: We will first formulate a (3+1)-dimensional integrable DDE candidate by incorporating discrete time delays into the structure of known equations like the combined pKP-BKP. The core technical approach is to adapt the Hirota bilinear method and the associated polynomial calculation algorithm to accommodate the delay terms. This involves defining a delayed bilinear operator and systematically testing for the existence of N-soliton solutions. We will then employ ansatz-based techniques (e.g., positive quadratic functions) to construct lump and traveling wave solutions, analyzing the impact of the delay parameter on their dynamics and interaction waveforms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic demonstration of deriving lump and multi-soliton solutions for a (3+1)-dimensional nonlinear DDE, significantly expanding the toolkit of analytical methods for delayed systems. It will yield new, exact solution families that explicitly show how time delays alter wave stability, interaction, and propagation in high-dimensional integrable models.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Wazwaz's recent work applies solution methods to standard (3+1)-D PDEs, the direct application and necessary algorithmic modification of the polynomial calculation method to incorporate explicit, continuous time delays in such high-dimensional systems is novel and non-trivial.

proposals.title: Reduced-Order Surrogate Models for Complex Oscillatory Systems via Bilinearization and Soliton Decomposition
proposals.motivation: Complex (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations, such as the pKP–BKP and Estevez-Prada models studied by Wazwaz, exhibit rich oscillatory and multi-soliton dynamics that are computationally expensive to simulate. Pre-2026, reduced-order modeling (ROM) techniques like Proper Orthogonal Decomposition are widely used for dissipative systems but are less effective for conservative, integrable systems with coherent structures. A method to generate accurate, low-dimensional surrogates for such systems would enable rapid exploration of parameter spaces and long-time simulations.
proposals.hypothesis: The bilinear Hirota formalism and associated soliton/wave solutions for integrable (3+1)-dimensional equations can be algorithmically leveraged to construct a physics-informed, low-dimensional basis, enabling the creation of efficient and accurate surrogate models that preserve key nonlinear interactions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the algorithm for generating multiple soliton and lump solutions, as used in Wazwaz's recent work, to systematically extract dominant coherent structures (solitons, periodic waves, lumps) as basis functions. These analytic solution forms will be used to initialize a nonlinear Galerkin projection framework, reducing the full PDE to a small system of ODEs for the time-dependent amplitudes. The specific steps involve: 1) applying the Hirota bilinear method to the target equation to identify solution ansatzes, 2) using a variant of the Petrov-Galerkin projection onto these analytic modes, and 3) calibrating the reduced model's coefficients against limited full-order simulation data via a sparse regression technique (akin to SINDy).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel ROM framework specifically tailored for high-dimensional, integrable nonlinear wave equations, extending ROM applicability beyond traditional fluid dynamics. It will deliver explicit, computationally cheap surrogate models capable of capturing soliton collisions and wave pattern interactions, providing a new tool for dynamical analysis and control in mathematical physics.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely integrates the analytic solution structures from integrability theory (the core of Wazwaz's expertise) directly into the construction of the reduced basis, moving beyond purely data-driven ROM methods. This creates a hybrid analytic-numeric surrogate that is non-obvious for high-dimensional systems.

proposals.title: Inverse Parameter Identification in (3+1)-Dimensional Integrable Systems via Soliton Decomposition and Optimization
proposals.motivation: The accurate identification of physical parameters (e.g., dispersion, nonlinearity coefficients) from observed wave patterns is a critical inverse problem in nonlinear physics and engineering. While direct methods for constructing soliton solutions in high-dimensional integrable equations are well-established, a systematic, solution-based framework for parameter estimation from experimental or numerical data remains underdeveloped, limiting the practical application of these models.
proposals.hypothesis: That the analytical structure of multi-soliton and lump solutions obtained via Hirota's bilinear method or symbolic computation can be leveraged within an optimization framework to uniquely and robustly identify the underlying system parameters from spatiotemporal oscillation data.
proposals.method: We will first derive a broad family of analytical solutions (lumps, solitons, breathers) for a target (3+1)-dimensional integrable equation (e.g., the combined pKP-BKP type) using Hirota's bilinear method and symbolic computation. A decomposition algorithm will then extract key waveform features (amplitude, velocity, phase) from observed data. These features will be matched to the analytical solution templates within a gradient-based or evolutionary optimization routine (e.g., Levenberg-Marquardt or Genetic Algorithm) to minimize the discrepancy and identify the optimal system parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mathematically rigorous pipeline for solving inverse problems in high-dimensional nonlinear systems, directly linking advanced soliton theory to data-driven parameter identification. It will extend the applicability of integrable models to practical scenarios where parameters are unknown or must be calibrated, bridging a gap between pure mathematical analysis and applied scientific computation.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines the detailed analytical solution catalog from integrability theory—a strength of the researcher's prior work—with inverse problem methodologies, moving beyond solution construction to system identification, which is non-trivial for complex (3+1)-dimensional equations.

proposals.title: Soliton Interactions and Wave Solutions for Integrable Equations with Discontinuous Kernels: A Hirota Bilinear and Lax Pair Approach
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in integrable systems, including Dr. Wazwaz's work, heavily relies on smooth kernels and solutions. However, physical phenomena like shock waves, boundary layers, and material defects necessitate models with discontinuous or singular kernels. Extending integrability methods to such non-smooth settings is a significant, open challenge with broad implications for nonlinear wave theory and applied mathematics.
proposals.hypothesis: That key integrable (3+1)-dimensional equations, such as the combined pKP-BKP or Estevez-Prada models, can be reformulated with distributional kernels while retaining essential structures like Lax pairs and bilinear forms, allowing for the construction of novel soliton and lump solutions with discontinuous derivatives.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Hirota bilinear method to accommodate kernels involving Heaviside step functions and Dirac delta distributions. This involves a careful distributional analysis of the bilinear operators D_x, D_y, D_z, D_t. We will then test for Painlevé integrability in the weak sense and seek a corresponding Lax pair formulation. The approach will be applied to specific (3+1)-D equations from the researcher's recent work to construct multi-soliton, lump, and traveling wave solutions with controlled singularities.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the analytical toolkit for integrable systems to a wider class of physically relevant, non-smooth problems. It will produce new solution types for established (3+1)-dimensional equations and provide a methodological framework for analyzing integrability under relaxed smoothness conditions.
proposals.novelty: While distributional solutions to PDEs are known, systematically applying the Hirota bilinear method and Lax pair formalism to integrable equations with explicitly discontinuous kernels is a novel synthesis, moving beyond the classical smoothness assumptions prevalent in the soliton literature pre-2026.

proposals.title: Lump, Soliton, and Traveling Wave Solutions for a Novel (3+1)-Dimensional Fractional-Order Integro-Differential Equation with Painlevé Integrability
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in nonlinear evolution equations has seen a surge in studying high-dimensional integrable systems and fractional calculus, but their combination remains underexplored. Fractional-order and integro-differential operators can model complex memory and non-local effects in wave phenomena, which are not captured by standard integer-order models. Extending the analysis of Painlevé-integrable equations to this hybrid framework is a crucial step for more realistic physical modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: That a new class of (3+1)-dimensional equations combining fractional derivatives and integral terms can pass the Painlevé integrability test, and that such equations will admit novel, analytically tractable solutions including fractional-order lumps, multi-solitons, and diverse traveling waves.
proposals.method: We will first formulate a candidate (3+1)-dimensional equation with multi-term fractional derivatives (using the conformable or Jumarie-modified Riemann-Liouville definitions, pre-2026 standards) and a convolution-type integral term. The Painlevé analysis will be adapted for fractional systems to establish integrability. For solutions, we will employ Hirota's bilinear method extended to fractional orders, the simplified homogeneous balance method, and the fractional sub-equation method to construct lump, multi-soliton, and periodic/traveling wave solutions. Numerical simulations will visualize the dynamical wave interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new integrable family of fractional integro-differential equations, expanding the known universe of solvable nonlinear models.
