It will provide a systematic methodology for deriving exact solutions in such hybrid systems and offer new waveform patterns that could describe anomalous dispersion and memory-laden wave processes in complex media.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of (3+1)-dimensional geometry, Painlevé integrability, fractional calculus, and integral operators into a single, solvable equation is a novel construct. The adaptation of solution techniques like the bilinear method to extract lump and soliton solutions from this combined fractional-integro-differential structure is non-trivial and unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Computational Schemes for Soliton Solutions of Higher-Dimensional Nonlinear Integral Equations
proposals.motivation: The study of integrable nonlinear systems in (3+1) dimensions is a frontier in mathematical physics, with direct implications for modeling complex wave phenomena in fields like fluid dynamics and nonlinear optics. While powerful analytical methods exist for differential equations, the systematic computational treatment of their integral equation counterparts in higher spatial dimensions remains underdeveloped, limiting the exploration of solution spaces and wave interaction patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: That the computational frameworks developed for obtaining soliton and lump solutions to (3+1)-dimensional nonlinear partial differential equations (PDEs) can be successfully adapted and extended to solve associated nonlinear integral equations, revealing new classes of multi-wave solutions and interaction dynamics.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Hirota bilinear method and symbolic computation tools, central to the researcher's recent work on PDEs, to handle integral operators in three spatial dimensions. The approach will involve formulating a bilinear form for target integral equations, such as those linked to the KP and BKP hierarchies. We will then algorithmically implement the simplified Hirota method and the positive quadratic function approach to derive N-soliton, lump, and hybrid solutions, using Mathematica or Maple for symbolic algebra and 3D/4D visualization of the resulting wave patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce new, explicit analytical solutions for a class of higher-dimensional nonlinear integral equations, extending the catalog of known solitonic structures. It will also provide a validated computational template for transitioning solution techniques from differential to integral formulations, thereby bridging two major classes of integrable systems.
proposals.novelty: The direct application of soliton computational schemes, like the simplified Hirota method, specifically to the integral equation forms of (3+1)-dimensional systems is a novel direction, moving beyond the well-trodden path of applying these methods solely to differential equations.

proposals.title: Dual Variational Principles for Nonlinear Integral Equations Arising in (3+1)-Dimensional Soliton Theory
proposals.motivation: The study of integrable (3+1)-dimensional nonlinear evolution equations, a core theme in Wazwaz's recent work, often leads to reformulations as nonlinear integral equations via inverse scattering or Hirota bilinear methods. Pre-2026, variational principles for such systems are underdeveloped, limiting the application of powerful analytical and numerical techniques like the variational iteration method (VIM) and Ritz approximations. Establishing a rigorous variational framework would provide a new, systematic tool for deriving and analyzing complex soliton and lump solutions.
proposals.hypothesis: That key classes of nonlinear integral equations associated with integrable (3+1)-dimensional PDEs (e.g., the combined pKP–BKP and Estevez-Prada equations) admit complementary (dual) Lagrangian formulations, enabling the construction of solution families via stationary action principles.
proposals.method: We will first derive the single variational principle by identifying a suitable Lagrangian density whose Euler-Lagrange equation recovers the target nonlinear integral equation, leveraging known bilinear forms. Subsequently, we will construct the dual variational principle using a Legendre-type transformation, defining a complementary functional whose stationary points correspond to the same solution set. The approach will be concretely applied to equations from Wazwaz's recent papers, using symbolic computation (Maple/Mathematica) to verify the consistency of the derived principles and to generate trial functions for approximating soliton waveforms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic variational foundation for several important (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations, extending the analytical toolkit beyond direct methods. It will enable new, robust numerical schemes based on the Ritz method applied to the dual functionals and offer a deeper theoretical understanding of the solution structure through conserved quantities linked to the Lagrangians.
proposals.novelty: While variational principles exist for some (1+1)-dimensional soliton equations, their formulation for the underlying nonlinear integral forms of (3+1)-dimensional systems is largely unexplored. The derivation of *dual* complementary principles for this class represents a significant and non-trivial extension of the variational approach to integrability.

proposals.title: Multi-Scale Soliton Dynamics and Interaction Patterns in a Novel Class of (3+1)-Dimensional Integro-Differential Equations
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in nonlinear integrable systems, particularly by Wazwaz, has extensively explored (3+1)-dimensional PDEs for soliton solutions. However, the analysis of equations where the nonlocality is governed by integral kernels exhibiting multiple, separated scales remains largely unexplored. Such multi-scale integral equations are crucial for modeling complex wave phenomena in plasmas and nonlinear optics where interactions occur across distinct spatial or temporal ranges, bridging a significant gap between pure PDE soliton theory and applied integral equation methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific class of (3+1)-dimensional integro-differential equations, derived from known integrable hierarchies but with a multi-scale singular kernel, will admit exact multi-soliton solutions and novel, stable interaction patterns governed by the scale separation parameter.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel integro-differential equation by merging a known (3+1)-dimensional integrable PDE (e.g., a combined KP-BKP type) with a convolution kernel of the form K(ε|x|) = exp(-|x|) + α exp(-|x|/ε), introducing a clear scale separation via a small parameter ε. The methodology will employ the Hirota bilinear formalism, adapted for integral terms, to derive N-soliton solutions. We will then analyze the asymptotic phase shifts and interaction patterns using direct substitution and graphical simulations, specifically studying the emergence of lump-like and breather structures from scale-interference.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of exact soliton solutions in a (3+1)-dimensional integro-differential system with explicit multi-scale kernels. It will extend the Painlevé integrability concept to a broader class of nonlocal equations and catalog new dynamical wave-form interaction patterns, offering a foundational framework for future studies in multi-scale nonlocal wave dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of Wazwaz's expertise in high-dimensional solitons with a structured multi-scale integral kernel, moving beyond standard PDEs to a hybrid system where soliton interactions are mediated by distinct nonlocal scales, a combination not previously investigated in the integrable systems literature.

proposals.title: Lump, Soliton, and Traveling Wave Solutions for (3+1)-Dimensional Painlevé-Integrable Equations with Variable Coefficients and Damping
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, the study of integrable equations like the pKP-BKP and Estevez-Prada models has been largely confined to constant-coefficient cases, which idealize physical reality. Many real-world phenomena in nonlinear optics, plasma physics, and fluid dynamics involve media with inhomogeneities and energy dissipation, modeled by variable coefficients and damping terms. Extending the analysis of Painlevé-integrable systems to such non-autonomous cases is crucial for bridging the gap between pure integrability theory and practical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: That the powerful analytical methods developed for constant-coefficient, integrable (3+1)-dimensional equations—specifically the Hirota bilinear method, simplified Hirota method, and ansatz-based techniques for lumps and traveling waves—can be systematically adapted to derive exact solutions for versions of these equations incorporating variable coefficients and non-constant damping.
proposals.method: We will first extend the Painlevé analysis to establish integrability constraints on the variable coefficients and damping functions in target equations like the combined pKP-BKP and Estevez-Prada models. Using the Hirota bilinear method, we will introduce a generalized bilinear transform with variable-coefficient-dependent auxiliary functions to construct N-soliton solutions. For lump and traveling wave solutions, we will employ specific polynomial and exponential ansätze within the simplified Hirota framework, treating the variable coefficients as slowly varying parameters to derive localized solutions. The symbolic computation will be performed using Mathematica to handle the increased algebraic complexity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new class of exact solutions for non-autonomous, (3+1)-dimensional integrable systems, explicitly showing how solitons, lumps, and traveling waves evolve in inhomogeneous and dissipative media. It will extend the applicability of Dr. Wazwaz's established solution techniques to more realistic physical models, providing a methodological template for analyzing variable-coefficient extensions of other integrable equations.
proposals.novelty: While variable-coefficient KdV and NLS equations have been studied, the systematic application of these advanced solution techniques—particularly for lump solutions and the Painlevé-integrable subclass—to (3+1)-dimensional equations with combined variable coefficients and damping is largely unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Soliton Dynamics and Novel Wave Solutions in a (3+1)-Dimensional Integrable Model with Fractional-Power Nonlinearity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on integrable systems, particularly by Wazwaz, heavily focuses on polynomial nonlinearities (e.g., quadratic, cubic). However, physical phenomena in optics, plasma physics, and Bose-Einstein condensates often involve saturable or higher-order nonlinearities not captured by simple polynomials. Extending the Painlevé integrability analysis and solution techniques to equations with non-polynomial terms, such as fractional-power nonlinearities (e.g., |u|^α u), remains a significant and underexplored challenge with direct implications for modeling more realistic nonlinear wave dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: That a (3+1)-dimensional extension of a known integrable hierarchy (e.g., the KP or BKP family) can be modified to include a fractional-power nonlinear term while retaining a form of Painlevé integrability, leading to new families of exact soliton, lump, and periodic wave solutions with altered interaction properties.
proposals.method: We will first construct a candidate (3+1)D equation by augmenting a known integrable model (e.g., the combined pKP-BKP) with a term like |u|^α u, where α is a positive real number. The Painlevé analysis (WTC test) will be systematically adapted for the fractional term, likely using a transformation u = v^p to handle the non-polynomiality. For solution construction, we will employ the Hirota bilinear method after a suitable transformation, alongside the simplified Hirota method and ansatz-based techniques (e.g., for lump and periodic waves) to derive multiple-soliton solutions and interaction patterns. Computational symbolic algebra (Maple/Mathematica) will be essential for managing the complex expansions.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the classification of integrable systems beyond polynomial nonlinearities, providing a new, solvable model for complex media. It will yield novel analytical solution structures (solitons, breathers, lumps) and characterize their collision dynamics, offering fresh insights into wave stability and interaction under generalized nonlinearity. The results will be directly applicable to advancing theoretical models in nonlinear physics.
proposals.novelty: The systematic integration of a fractional-power nonlinear term into the framework of (3+1)-dimensional Painlevé-integrable equations and the subsequent derivation of exact solutions represents a clear departure from the standard polynomial-assumption literature dominant before 2026.

proposals.title: Small-Amplitude Limits and Weakly Nonlinear Approximations of (3+1)-Dimensional Painlevé-Integrable Equations
proposals.motivation: The study of exact soliton solutions in (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations, such as the pKP-BKP and Estevez-Prada models, is well-established. However, the connection between these fully nonlinear, localized structures and the linear or weakly nonlinear wave regimes that dominate many physical contexts remains underexplored. Systematically deriving these approximations is crucial for linking integrable systems theory to real-world wave phenomena in fluid dynamics and nonlinear optics, where amplitudes are often small.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a consistent small-amplitude scaling applied to recently studied (3+1)-dimensional Painlevé-integrable equations will yield canonical linear wave equations or weakly nonlinear models (like KdV-type equations in a moving frame), and that the known multi-soliton solutions will reduce to superpositions of these linear/weakly nonlinear modes in the limit.
proposals.method: We will apply a formal multiple-scale perturbation method. First, we introduce a small parameter ε scaling the amplitude of the field in the exact soliton solutions (e.g., u → εu) for equations like the combined pKP-BKP and the Estevez-Prada equation. We will then perform an asymptotic expansion of the equation and its known Hirota bilinear form. At O(ε), we will derive the linear dispersion relation and plane wave solutions. At O(ε^2) or O(ε^3), we will obtain the governing nonlinear Schrödinger or KdV-type equation for the slowly modulating envelope, using a reductive perturbation technique in a co-moving coordinate frame.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clear asymptotic bridge between complex (3+1)-D integrable systems and more tractable, physically intuitive wave equations. It will classify the linear and weakly nonlinear regimes emergent from these systems and demonstrate how exact solitons morph into linear wave packets or solitary waves of the reduced models. This strengthens the physical interpretation and applicability of high-dimensional soliton theory.
proposals.novelty: While small-amplitude limits are standard for (1+1)-D equations, their systematic application to the new class of (3+1)-D Painlevé-integrable equations studied by Wazwaz is novel. The focus on extracting both linear wave physics and weakly nonlinear models directly from the structure of known multi-soliton solutions, rather than just the bare equation, provides a fresh, solution-informed perspective.

proposals.title: Formulation and Application of the Inverse Scattering Transform for the Modified Kaup-Kupershmidt Equation
proposals.motivation: The modified Kaup-Kupershmidt (mKK) equation is a significant fifth-order integrable nonlinear evolution equation known to admit soliton and other complex solutions. While direct methods like Hirota's bilinear form and ansatz-based approaches have been used to construct its solutions, a complete inverse scattering transform (IST) framework for the mKK equation remains undeveloped. Establishing this framework is crucial for a rigorous understanding of its complete integrability, the spectral properties of its solutions, and for solving initial-value problems, which is a cornerstone of soliton theory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the modified Kaup-Kupershmidt equation possesses a Lax pair suitable for a well-posed inverse scattering problem, and that its associated scattering data can be characterized to reconstruct multi-soliton and radiative solutions via a Gel'fand-Levitan-Marchenko-type integral equation.
proposals.method: The research will first derive and validate a suitable Lax pair for the mKK equation. We will then perform a spectral analysis of the associated linear scattering problem to determine the analytic properties of the Jost functions and the scattering data (reflection coefficient, discrete eigenvalues, and normalization constants). The core technical task will be to formulate the inverse problem as a Riemann-Hilbert problem or a system of linear integral equations. This will be solved using established IST algorithms to explicitly reconstruct potential solutions, with a focus on pure N-soliton solutions corresponding to a vanishing reflection coefficient.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first complete inverse scattering transform for the modified Kaup-Kupershmidt equation, solidifying its status as a fully integrable system. It will yield a systematic method for solving its initial-value problem and offer deep spectral insights into its soliton dynamics, beyond what is possible with direct construction methods alone.
proposals.novelty: While the IST for the standard KdV and Kaup-Kupershmidt equations is classical, the formulation for the *modified* KK equation is non-trivial and unexplored, requiring a novel analysis of its distinct Lax structure and scattering data to address its higher nonlinearity and different symmetry properties.

proposals.title: Inverse Classification of Nonlinear Evolution Equations via Sine-Cosine Ansatz: A Systematic Approach to Deriving Admissible PDEs
proposals.motivation: The standard approach in nonlinear wave theory is to find solutions for a given equation. The inverse problem—determining the class of equations that admit a specific, physically meaningful solution form—is less explored but crucial for model identification and understanding solution universality. Given the prevalence of sine-cosine type solutions in describing periodic and solitary wave phenomena across physics, a systematic framework for this inverse problem is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given sine-cosine ansatz (e.g., u(x,t) = A sin^k(μξ) or cos^k(μξ), ξ = x - ct), there exists a definable, potentially integrable, class of (3+1)-dimensional nonlinear evolution equations that can be algorithmically derived by enforcing the ansatz as a solution.
proposals.method: We will employ a symbolic computation approach. First, a general polynomial-type nonlinear evolution equation in (3+1)D with arbitrary coefficients and derivative terms (e.g., u_t, u_x, u_xx, u_xxx, u_xy, u_xt, u^2, u u_x) will be posited. The prescribed sine-cosine ansatz and its derivatives will be substituted. Using tools like Maple or Mathematica, we will collect coefficients of like trigonometric terms (sin^m(μξ) cos^n(μξ)) and set them to zero. This generates an overdetermined algebraic system for the equation's unknown coefficients and parameters (A, μ, c, k), which will be solved to classify all possible admissible equations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, algorithmic methodology for the inverse classification of PDEs, moving beyond solution-finding to equation-finding. It will generate new, potentially integrable (3+1)-dimensional equations that share common solution structures, enriching the pool of models for physical applications. The framework will also test the robustness of the sine-cosine ansatz across equation families.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Wazwaz's recent work finds solutions for given equations, this proposal inverts the paradigm. It systematically derives equations from a solution ansatz, a non-trivial extension of the Painlevé analysis and direct methods he employs, applied specifically to the high-dimensional, integrable-like systems central to his research.

proposals.title: A Symbolic Computation Framework for Automated Hirota Bilinearization and Soliton Solution Construction of (3+1)-Dimensional Painlevé-Integrable Equations
proposals.motivation: Hirota's bilinear method is a powerful technique for deriving exact soliton solutions of integrable nonlinear evolution equations, but its application to complex (3+1)-dimensional equations is algebraically intensive and error-prone. Manual derivation of the necessary bilinear forms, auxiliary functions, and solution constraints for equations like the combined pKP–BKP or Estevez-Prada models is a significant bottleneck, limiting systematic exploration. Automating this process would accelerate the discovery and classification of new soliton and wave interaction patterns in higher-dimensional integrable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a rule-based symbolic computation architecture can algorithmically perform the Hirota bilinearization of a given (3+1)-dimensional Painlevé-integrable equation, systematically determine the associated auxiliary functions and resonance parameters, and subsequently generate multi-soliton and lump-type solutions.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational algebra package in *Mathematica* or *Maple*. The core algorithm will: 1) Accept a nonlinear PDE and perform a Painlevé integrability test (WTC method) to confirm suitability. 2) Implement a rule-based search for a dependent variable transformation (e.g., u = 2(ln f)_xx) to convert the equation into a Hirota bilinear form D_t, D_x, D_y, D_z. 3) Use symbolic polynomial solving to determine the dispersion relations and phase shifts for N-soliton solutions. 4) Extend the framework to automate the construction of lump solutions by imposing specific complex conjugate constraints on parameters within the positive quadratic function f.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-of-its-kind open-source computational tool for the automated bilinear analysis of (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations. It will generate new, verifiable soliton and lump solutions for targeted models like the extended fifth-order and Estevez-Prada equations, providing deeper insight into their wave dynamics. The framework will serve as a foundational resource for researchers studying integrability and nonlinear wave phenomena.
proposals.novelty: While symbolic computation has been used for Painlevé analysis, a fully automated, end-to-end system for Hirota bilinearization and solution generation tailored to the complexities of (3+1)-dimensional equations represents a significant advance over existing, piecemeal software tools.

proposals.title: Renormalization Group Analysis of Asymptotic Soliton Dynamics in (3+1)-Dimensional Painlevé-Integrable Equations
proposals.motivation: The renormalization group (RG) method, developed in quantum field theory and critical phenomena, has proven effective for deriving asymptotic solutions to nonlinear differential equations, particularly in low-dimensional systems. However, its application to complex (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations, which are central to modeling nonlinear wave phenomena in fluids and plasmas, remains largely unexplored. This project aims to bridge this gap, leveraging the RG's power to systematically extract long-time, large-scale behavior from singular perturbation problems inherent in soliton interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the renormalization group perturbation method can be systematically adapted to derive and unify the asymptotic forms of multi-soliton, lump, and interaction wave-form solutions for high-dimensional Painlevé-integrable equations, providing a more robust framework than direct ansatz methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RG perturbation algorithm, as formalized by Chen, Goldenfeld, and Oono, to selected (3+1)-dimensional Painlevé-integrable models (e.g., the combined pKP-BKP or Estevez-Prada equations). The concrete steps are: 1) Introduce a formal perturbation parameter into the equation and obtain a naive perturbation series solution. 2) Identify and regularize secular terms that cause unbounded growth. 3) Introduce renormalization constants and derive the RG equation, which is an evolution equation for the integration 'constants' of the naive solution. 4) Solve the RG equation to obtain the uniformly valid asymptotic solution, interpreting the results as soliton parameters (amplitude, phase, velocity) evolving due to interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, systematic derivation of asymptotic soliton dynamics in (3+1)D, moving beyond solution construction via ansatz. It will yield a new analytical tool for the integrable systems community, capable of handling complex interaction patterns and potentially predicting new asymptotic wave forms. The results will deepen the understanding of the connection between renormalization group philosophy and integrability.
proposals.novelty: While RG techniques have been applied to some nonlinear PDEs, their targeted use for deriving asymptotic *soliton* and *lump* solutions in high-dimensional, Painlevé-integrable equations—a core focus of Dr. Wazwaz's recent work—is novel. It transfers a powerful physics-based asymptotic method into a domain dominated by direct ansatz and Hirota's bilinear approaches.

proposals.title: Inverse Determination of Nonlinear Coefficients in (3+1)-Dimensional Integrable Equations from Observed Soliton Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The direct construction of soliton solutions for integrable equations with variable coefficients is well-studied, but the inverse problem—deducing the governing equation's parameters from observed wave properties—remains largely unexplored. This capability is crucial for modeling complex nonlinear phenomena in fluid dynamics and nonlinear optics, where the underlying physical laws may be inferred from experimental wave data. Addressing this inverse problem would bridge a significant gap between theoretical integrability and practical system identification.
proposals.hypothesis: The nonlinear coefficients and dispersion parameters of a broad class of (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations (e.g., combined pKP–BKP type) can be uniquely recovered by analyzing the amplitude, velocity, and interaction phase shifts of their multi-soliton solutions. We hypothesize that a systematic mapping exists between these observable soliton properties and the equation's constitutive coefficients.
proposals.method: We will formulate the inverse problem as an optimization task. First, we will derive general symbolic expressions for N-soliton solution properties (wave numbers, frequencies, phase shifts) for a parameterized (3+1)-D equation using Hirota's bilinear method. Then, we will develop a computational algorithm that uses a gradient-descent or genetic algorithm to minimize the difference between observed soliton properties and those predicted by the symbolic model. The algorithm will be tested on synthetic data generated from known equations like the Estevez-Prada and fifth-order Painlevé-integrable models to validate coefficient recovery.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for the inverse scattering transform in a modern context, applied to high-dimensional systems. It will deliver a concrete methodology for physicists to infer governing equations from laboratory or numerical observations of complex wave patterns, moving beyond mere solution construction.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Wazwaz's prior work focuses on generating solutions for given equations, this proposal inverts the paradigm to extract the equation from the solutions. The application of systematic parameter optimization to the inverse problem for (3+1)-dimensional integrable systems, based on multi-soliton interaction data, is a novel and non-trivial extension of his research trajectory.

proposals.title: Lump, Soliton, and Interaction Solutions for a New (4+1)-Dimensional Integrable KP-Type Equation
proposals.motivation: The Kadomtsev–Petviashvili (KP) equation is a cornerstone of integrable systems describing nonlinear waves in (2+1) dimensions. Extending its integrable hierarchy to higher spatial dimensions (3+1 and beyond) remains a challenging frontier, with profound implications for modeling complex wave phenomena in theoretical physics and fluid dynamics. Pre-2026 work has introduced several (3+1)-dimensional integrable KP-type equations, but systematic analysis of their (4+1)-dimensional generalizations is largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a new (4+1)-dimensional KP-type equation can be constructed via Painlevé integrability analysis, and that it will admit novel, localized solutions—including lumps, multiple solitons, and resonant wave structures—that exhibit richer interaction dynamics than their lower-dimensional counterparts.
proposals.method: We will first employ the Painlevé integrability test (WTC method) to derive a new (4+1)-dimensional integrable extension of the KP hierarchy. The Hirota bilinear method will then be applied to construct N-soliton solutions. For lump and other localized solutions, we will use the positive quadratic function approach within the Hirota framework. Symbolic computation (Maple/Mathematica) will be essential for handling the high-dimensional algebra. Finally, graphical analysis will be performed to study the dynamical interactions and wave-form patterns of the obtained solutions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, integrable (4+1)-dimensional model, significantly expanding the known family of high-dimensional KP equations. It will provide a catalog of exact solutions (lumps, solitons, breathers) and characterize their interaction properties, offering new testbeds for the theory of integrable systems in higher dimensions.
proposals.novelty: While (3+1)-dimensional KP-type equations have been studied, a systematic construction and solution analysis for a (4+1)-dimensional integrable case is novel. The focus on deriving *lump* solutions in a (4+1)D setting, alongside complex soliton interactions, goes beyond most existing pre-2026 studies.

proposals.title: Analytical Soliton and Lump Solutions for Novel Integro-Differential Models via an Extended Adomian Decomposition Method
proposals.motivation: Integro-differential equations (IDEs) are crucial for modeling phenomena with non-local effects and memory, prevalent in viscoelasticity, population dynamics, and plasma physics. However, analytical solution techniques for high-dimensional, nonlinear IDEs, especially those with integrable structures, remain underdeveloped compared to pure differential equations. Extending powerful soliton solution methods to this broader class is a significant pre-2026 challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: The Adomian Decomposition Method (ADM) framework, when systematically extended and combined with integrability analysis, can generate novel analytical solutions—including solitons, lumps, and traveling waves—for newly constructed, physically relevant integro-differential equations.
proposals.method: We will construct novel (3+1)-dimensional integro-differential equations by introducing non-local integral terms into known integrable models like the KP and BKP hierarchies. The standard ADM will be extended by deriving new Adomian polynomials for the integral operators. The method will be applied to decompose the solution into a series, with the recursion scheme initialized using a hypothesized soliton or lump ansatz. Integrability will be tested via the Painlevé analysis adapted for IDEs. Symbolic computation (Maple/Mathematica) will be used to handle the complex algebra.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new solution methodology for a challenging class of equations and yield the first reported analytical soliton and lump solutions for several proposed integro-differential models. It will expand the known landscape of integrable systems and provide benchmark solutions for testing numerical solvers.
proposals.novelty: The direct application and extension of the ADM to derive soliton solutions for *integro-differential* versions of high-dimensional integrable equations is a novel synthesis. Prior work by the researcher and others focuses on differential equations; this explicitly bridges the gap to non-local operators.

proposals.title: Soliton Collisions and Wave-Form Patterns in a Spatially Inhomogeneous (3+1)-Dimensional Combined pKP–BKP Equation
proposals.motivation: Most studies of soliton interactions, including Dr. Wazwaz's recent work, assume constant-coefficient integrable equations, preserving translational invariance. Real-world media, such as plasmas with density gradients or optical fibers with varying refractive indices, are spatially inhomogeneous, breaking this symmetry. Understanding how soliton collisions and wave-form patterns are modified in such settings is crucial for applications in nonlinear optics, fluid dynamics, and Bose-Einstein condensates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatially varying coefficients in a (3+1)-dimensional integrable model will fundamentally alter soliton collision dynamics—including phase shifts, amplitudes, and the stability of complex interaction patterns—compared to the homogeneous case, potentially inducing novel resonant or trapped states.
proposals.method: We will extend the (3+1)-dimensional combined pKP–BKP equation by introducing slowly varying spatial inhomogeneities into its coefficients. The primary analytical tool will be a perturbed Hirota bilinear method, treating the inhomogeneity as a small perturbation to the integrable structure. We will derive modified N-soliton solutions and analyze phase shifts post-collision. For stronger inhomogeneities, we will employ symbolic computation and direct numerical simulation (e.g., pseudo-spectral methods) to study the dynamical evolution of soliton collisions and the persistence of lump and other wave-form patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for studying soliton interactions in non-uniform media for high-dimensional integrable systems. It will yield new classes of analytical and numerical solutions, charting how fundamental properties like elasticity and superposition are broken or preserved. The results will advance the theoretical understanding of nonlinear waves in realistic, inhomogeneous environments.
proposals.novelty: While inhomogeneous extensions exist for (1+1)-dimensional systems, applying this analysis to the complex, multi-soliton and lump solutions of a (3+1)-dimensional combined integrable equation is a significant and unexplored step. The focus on collision dynamics, rather than just single-soliton propagation, in such a high-dimensional inhomogeneous context is novel.

proposals.title: Soliton-Radiation Interactions in (3+1)-Dimensional Painlevé Integrable Equations: Dynamics and Waveform Patterns
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on integrable equations, including Dr. Wazwaz's work, predominantly analyzes pure soliton-soliton interactions. In real-world applications, solitons are rarely isolated; they interact with non-solitary wave components like radiative tails or continuous spectra. Understanding these interactions is crucial for modeling wave phenomena in nonlinear optics and fluid dynamics more accurately.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for (3+1)-dimensional Painlevé integrable equations, the interaction between solitons and non-solitary wave initial conditions will generate persistent, structured waveform patterns, and that the soliton's core properties (speed, amplitude) will be modified in a predictable way by the radiative background.
proposals.method: We will extend the Hirota bilinear method to construct solutions for equations like the combined pKP-BKP and Estevez-Prada equations, starting with initial conditions combining an N-soliton solution with a small-amplitude, spatially extended wave packet (representing the continuous spectrum). The dynamics will be analyzed using symbolic computation and direct numerical simulation (e.g., pseudo-spectral methods) to track energy exchange and pattern evolution. The Painlevé integrability test will guide the selection of equations to ensure analytical tractability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic classification of interaction outcomes (e.g., adiabatic soliton deformation, resonant radiation shedding) for high-dimensional integrable systems. It will extend the analytical toolkit for non-pure soliton initial data and offer new benchmark results for testing numerical solvers in nonlinear wave theory.
proposals.novelty: While soliton-soliton interactions are well-studied, a focused analysis of soliton interactions with deliberately introduced radiative components in (3+1)-dimensional Painlevé integrable equations is novel. It moves beyond the standard multi-soliton solution ansatz to address more physically realistic initial conditions.

proposals.title: Variational Collective Coordinate Analysis of Soliton Interactions in (3+1)-Dimensional Painlevé-Integrable Equations
proposals.motivation: The study of (3+1)-dimensional integrable equations, such as the combined pKP–BKP and Estevez-Prada models, has revealed rich families of exact soliton and lump solutions. However, analytical descriptions of their dynamical interactions—especially head-on collisions, bound states, and resonant phenomena—remain limited to specific ansätze. A systematic, approximate method for modeling the full interaction dynamics is needed to complement exact solution techniques and enhance predictive understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: That the complex interaction dynamics of solitons in high-dimensional Painlevé-integrable systems can be accurately captured by a low-dimensional Lagrangian system derived via a variational collective coordinate approach, with time-dependent parameters representing amplitude, position, width, and phase.
proposals.method: We will apply a reduced variational principle to the Lagrangian density of target equations (e.g., the Estevez-Prada equation). A judiciously chosen trial function, incorporating multiple solitary wave profiles with time-dependent collective coordinates (positions, amplitudes, widths), will be substituted. The resulting effective Lagrangian, integrated over the spatial coordinates, yields a set of coupled ordinary differential equations (ODEs) for the coordinates via the Euler-Lagrange equations. These ODEs will be solved numerically to model interactions beyond the regime of exact multi-soliton solutions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, adaptable analytical framework for approximating soliton interaction dynamics in (3+1)-dimensional integrable systems where full analytical interaction solutions are unknown. It will yield quantitative predictions for phase shifts, radiation, and stability during collisions, bridging the gap between exact solitary wave solutions and full numerical simulation.
proposals.novelty: While collective coordinate methods are established in (1+1)-D systems, their application to the nuanced soliton and lump interactions in (3+1)-D Painlevé-integrable equations, particularly for models recently studied by the author, is novel and non-trivial, moving beyond direct solution construction to dynamical modeling.

proposals.title: Exact Solutions for (3+1)-Dimensional Integrable Equations via Generalized Traveling Wave Reductions with Variable Coefficients
proposals.motivation: The standard traveling wave ansatz, which reduces a PDE to a constant-coefficient ODE, is a major limitation in soliton theory. It restricts solutions to a single direction of propagation and cannot capture more complex, multi-directional, or spatially modulated wave interactions that are physically relevant. Developing methods that relax this constraint is crucial for advancing the study of nonlinear dynamics in higher-dimensional integrable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing a generalized traveling wave reduction with variable coefficients, such as an ansatz of the form u(x,y,z,t) = U(ξ) where ξ = p(x,y,z,t) is a non-linear function, it is possible to derive exact solutions—including solitons, lumps, and periodic waves—for (3+1)-dimensional integrable PDEs that are inaccessible via the standard constant-speed transform.
proposals.method: We will apply the generalized ansatz to key (3+1)-dimensional Painlevé-integrable equations from Dr. Wazwaz's recent work, such as the combined pKP–BKP and Estevez-Prada equations. The method involves substituting the ansatz into the PDE, using symbolic computation (e.g., Maple or Mathematica) to separate variables, and solving the resulting system of determining equations for the variable coefficient functions p(x,y,z,t) and the profile U(ξ). Techniques like the Hirota bilinear method and simplified Hirota derivatives will be adapted to this variable-coefficient framework to construct multi-soliton and lump solutions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new class of exact solutions for important (3+1)-dimensional integrable models, revealing novel wave interaction patterns and localized structures. It will extend the analytical toolkit for nonlinear wave theory, providing a systematic pathway to study waves with directionally dependent speeds and amplitudes in complex media.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond the ubiquitous constant-coefficient ODE reduction, directly targeting the generation of solutions with inherent spatial and temporal variability in their propagation characteristics, a significant departure from most pre-2026 solution techniques in the field.

proposals.title: Inverse Classification of Integrable Equations: Deriving (3+1)-Dimensional PDEs from Specified Solitary Wave and Lump Solutions
proposals.motivation: The direct search for exact solutions to nonlinear PDEs is a central theme in integrable systems, but the inverse problem—determining which equations admit a given physically or mathematically significant waveform—is largely unexplored. This inverse approach, grounded in pre-2026 soliton theory, could systematically uncover new integrable or partially integrable equations in higher dimensions and clarify the solution space's structure.
proposals.hypothesis: By imposing a specific ansatz (e.g., a sech^2 solitary wave or a rational lump form) as a solution constraint on a general, multi-dimensional polynomial PDE ansatz, a systematic algebraic procedure can determine the unique class of equations, including integrable hierarchies, for which the specified function is an exact solution.
proposals.method: We will adopt and extend the Painlevé analysis framework used by Wazwaz. For a given solution form u_0(x,y,z,t), we substitute it into a general ansatz for a (3+1)-dimensional equation with undetermined coefficients and nonlinearity orders (e.g., a combination of KP and BKP-type terms). Requiring the resulting condition to hold identically generates an overdetermined algebraic system for the coefficients. We will solve this using symbolic computation (Maple/Mathematica) and consistency conditions, potentially employing a weight-based method to limit the general ansatz's complexity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, systematic method for generating new (3+1)-dimensional PDEs with known exact solutions, potentially expanding known integrable families. It will provide a foundational inverse technique for the field, moving beyond *ad hoc* solution methods to a more classification-oriented paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While solution techniques are mature, the explicit, constructive inversion from a prescribed solution to a family of governing equations for (3+1)-dimensional systems is a non-trivial and largely unaddressed problem. It inverts the standard methodology of integrable systems research.

ideas.title: Multi-Scale Analysis of Non-Separable Integral Kernels
ideas.description: This research direction develops a theory and computational methods for integral equations where the kernel is both non-separable and exhibits multiple, distinct scales (e.g., rapidly oscillating, having boundary layers, or containing fine local features). Instead of treating non-separability as a monolithic obstacle, the approach decomposes the problem according to its inherent scale hierarchy. The core synergy is the creation of structured, scale-adapted approximations—such as multi-resolution dictionaries or coupled asymptotic expansions—that can capture the kernel's complex, non-factorable behavior where traditional separable decompositions fail. This enables accurate and efficient solutions for a new class of multi-scale problems in wave propagation, homogenization, and non-local physics.

ideas.title: Homotopy Analysis of Nonlinear Vector-Borne Disease Models with Seasonally-Varying Integral Kernels
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the need for seasonal forcing in blackfly-borne disease models with the systematic application of the Homotopy Analysis Method (HAM). It develops a framework where the nonlinear integral equations governing parasite dynamics feature time-dependent, periodic transmission kernels. HAM is adapted to handle these non-autonomous integral operators, constructing analytical approximations that capture the interplay between intrinsic nonlinearities and extrinsic seasonal drivers. This provides a powerful tool for probing multi-stability, bifurcations, and long-term periodic solutions in a more analytically transparent way than full numerical computation allows.

ideas.title: Hybrid Spectral-Finite Element Methods for Singular Integral Equations via Multiscale Domain Decomposition
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to develop hybrid computational methods for singular integral equations by drawing an analogy from computational seismology's approach to multiscale wave propagation. The analogous problem of modeling seismic waves across different physical scales is solved by hybridizing spectral-element and finite-difference methods within a domain decomposition framework. Adapting this solution, the research will focus on partitioning the integration domain for a singular integral equation based on the behavior of its kernel. A high-accuracy, specialized scheme (e.g., adaptive spectral or singularity-subtraction method) will handle the challenging near-singularity region, while a computationally efficient standard method will handle the remaining far-field. The core innovation will be the design of a mathematically consistent and numerically stable coupling mechanism at the interface between these regions, aiming to achieve superior overall accuracy and efficiency for problems with strongly singular kernels compared to using a single method across the entire domain.

ideas.title: Spectral-Collocation Framework for High-Accuracy Bifurcation Analysis of Integral Equations with General Kernels
ideas.description: This research develops a novel computational framework for bifurcation analysis by integrating spectral collocation methods directly into the systematic search for critical points in nonlinear integral equations. The approach uses high-order spectral discretization to accurately represent complex, smooth kernels, transforming the infinite-dimensional eigenvalue problem associated with linearized stability into a finite, highly accurate matrix eigenvalue problem. This synergy allows for the efficient and reliable detection of bifurcations—such as Hopf or pitchfork—in systems with general kernels where traditional low-order discretizations fail or require excessive resolution. The resulting methodology provides a powerful tool for exploring nonlinear dynamics in applications ranging from nonlocal models in biology to mean-field models in statistical physics.

ideas.title: Adapt Homotopy Analysis for Nonlinear Integral Equations via Continuation Methods from Structural Engineering
ideas.description: This idea systematically adapts the homotopy analysis method (HAM) to nonlinear integral equations by drawing a direct analogy from solution techniques in structural engineering. The analogous problem is solving nonlinear structural equilibrium equations, which is tackled using arc-length continuation methods—a form of numerical homotopy that uses predictor-corrector schemes to trace solution paths. The adapted research idea transfers this predictor-corrector, path-following logic to the analytical domain of nonlinear integral equations. It proposes constructing a deliberate homotopy deformation of the integral operator, using a predictor step to generate an initial guess from a tractable baseline equation, and a corrector step involving spectral or iterative numerical methods to converge to the solution of the target equation. This approach aims to provide a more robust and systematic framework for applying HAM to integral equations, improving convergence control and enabling the analysis of solution branches and bifurcations, much like tracing load-displacement curves in engineering.

ideas.title: Multi-scale integral equations solved via homogenization and renormalization group techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts solution techniques from condensed matter physics and quantum field theory—specifically renormalization group (RG) flow and homogenization—to the study of integral equations with kernels or solutions exhibiting multiple, separated scales. The analogous problem involves deriving effective large-scale theories from systems with microscopic details, solved by progressively coarse-graining the description. The adapted approach treats the multi-scale integral equation as defining an operator whose properties change with an introduced scale parameter. By constructing an RG transformation that integrates out high-frequency or fine-scale components, the method aims to derive a flow equation for the kernel. This flow, from fine to coarse scales, should yield a simplified, effective integral equation valid at the largest scale, while also potentially providing matched asymptotic expansions by preserving information about the eliminated scales through boundary conditions or matching procedures. The goal is to develop a systematic analytical and numerical framework for solving a class of currently intractable multi-scale integral equations.

ideas.title: SpectralIE: A High-Performance, High-Order Spectral Framework for General Integral Equations
ideas.description: This project develops an open-source software package, SpectralIE, which provides a comprehensive, high-performance implementation of spectral collocation methods for a wide range of integral equations. The core innovation is a modular architecture that cleanly separates the high-order discretization of integral operators, the fast evaluation of resulting dense systems via spectral acceleration techniques, and the solvers for the linear systems. This design allows researchers to easily prototype new spectral schemes and hybrid methods while providing engineers with a robust, well-documented toolbox for solving challenging problems in electromagnetics, acoustics, and fluid dynamics with unprecedented accuracy and efficiency.

ideas.title: HAM with Automated Convergence Control for Nonlinear Integral Equations via Surrogate Optimization
ideas.description: This research develops a robust, automated variant of the Homotopy Analysis Method (HAM) specifically for nonlinear integral equations. The core innovation is the integration of a machine learning-guided optimization framework that systematically determines the optimal auxiliary parameters (like the convergence-control parameter ℏ) required by HAM. By training a surrogate model on the residual error of the integral equation and iteratively refining parameter choices via Bayesian optimization, the method eliminates the heuristic guesswork that traditionally plagues HAM. This results in a reliably convergent, computationally efficient solver for a broader and more complex class of nonlinear integral equations than previously possible with standard HAM.

ideas.title: Apply Padé approximants to nonlinear integral models via a rational closure method from turbulence modeling
ideas.description: This idea extends the use of Padé approximants to a wider class of nonlinear integral models by drawing an analogy from fluid dynamics. In turbulence modeling, the closure problem for nonlinear Reynolds stresses is often addressed by constructing rational approximations (e.g., algebraic stress models) that represent complex nonlinearities as functions of mean strain rates. Adapting this solution, we propose treating the nonlinear components of integral models (e.g., in Volterra or Hammerstein equations) as targets for rational closure. The research would develop a methodology to derive Padé approximants for these nonlinear integral operators, where the numerator and denominator are composed of iterated linear integral operators. This approach aims to deliver more accurate, stable, and convergent approximations for strongly nonlinear applications where traditional perturbation or polynomial series fail, potentially impacting fields like kinetic theory, nonlocal elasticity, and biological aggregation models.

ideas.title: Adapting Scattering Theory from Quantum Physics to Solve Equations on Unbounded Domains
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to extend solution techniques for equations to unbounded or complex domains by drawing an analogy from quantum scattering theory. In that field, solving the Schrödinger equation on infinite domains for scattering states is achieved by decomposing the wavefunction into an incident and a scattered component, then focusing computational effort on the localized scattered part using techniques like complex scaling or perfectly matched layers to handle the infinite exterior. Adapted to general mathematical equations (e.g., PDEs on unbounded spatial domains, integral equations on infinite lines, or equations in complex plane sectors), the idea is to develop a framework that: 1) identifies a suitable asymptotic 'free solution' for a given equation and domain, 2) reformulates the problem in terms of a correction term with desirable decay properties, and 3) employs domain-transforming or absorbing boundary techniques to numerically solve for this correction on a truncated, compact region. This approach challenges the assumption that equations must be posed on compact domains or simple regions for numerical treatment, potentially enabling efficient solution of problems in wave propagation, exterior calculus, and complex analysis.

ideas.title: Well-Posed Formulation and Adaptive Discretization for Equations on Non-Compact Domains
ideas.description: This research develops a rigorous computational framework for solving PDEs and integral equations on unbounded or geometrically complex domains. It begins by deriving physically/mathematically correct well-posed formulations, including precise function space settings and generalized boundary conditions. The core of the approach is the co-design of adaptive numerical schemes—such as infinite elements, perfectly matched layers, or function-adapted spectral methods—that are provably stable and convergent within the established well-posed framework. This ensures that computed solutions are meaningful approximations of the true, uniquely defined solution.

ideas.title: Automated Hybrid Solver Synthesis with Cost-Accuracy Trade-off Analysis for Multi-Scale Integral Equations
ideas.description: This research develops a systematic framework for generating and evaluating hybrid numerical-asymptotic solvers for integral equations with separated scales. The core methodology involves first using symbolic asymptotic analysis to decompose the kernel and identify dominant contributions at each scale. These analytical insights then automatically inform the synthesis of a hybrid algorithm, which strategically combines high-resolution numerics for fine-scale features with fast asymptotic approximations for coarse-scale behavior. The system rigorously quantifies the computational cost and solution error of the synthesized solver as a function of scale separation parameters, producing clear performance maps to guide practitioners in selecting and tuning methods for specific problems.

ideas.title: Hybrid Computational Methods for Stochastic Singular Integral Equations
ideas.description: This research direction develops novel hybrid numerical-analytical schemes specifically designed for integral equations featuring both strongly singular kernels and stochastic elements (e.g., random coefficients or forcing). It combines techniques like singularity subtraction or quadrature with stochastic Galerkin or collocation methods, creating a unified computational strategy. The core challenge and novelty lie in managing the interaction between the high gradients from singularities and the probabilistic dimensions, requiring adaptive hybridizations. This enables reliable uncertainty quantification for critical applications in fracture mechanics, wave propagation in random media, and financial models with singular jump kernels.

ideas.title: Regularization-Path Coupled Discretizations for Non-Smooth Integral Equations
ideas.description: This research develops a rigorous computational framework for integral equations with discontinuous or distributional kernels by explicitly coupling solution regularization with numerical discretization. The core innovation is a provably stable 'regularization-to-discretization' pipeline, where a family of regularized problems defines the solution concept, and adaptive multi-resolution discretizations are constructed to converge along this path. This approach yields well-defined solution concepts for broad classes of non-smooth kernels and provides constructive, convergent numerical algorithms with rigorous error and stability guarantees, overcoming the foundational ill-posedness of the original formulation.

ideas.title: Adapting N-Body Simulation Hierarchical Methods to High-Dimensional Integral Equations
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to extend computational schemes to integral equations in three or more spatial dimensions by drawing an analogy from computational astrophysics. The analogous problem is the N-body problem in gravitational simulations, where direct calculation of pairwise interactions is prohibitively expensive for large N. This was solved using hierarchical tree algorithms (like Barnes-Hut) and the Fast Multipole Method, which group distant particles into clusters and approximate their aggregate influence using multipole expansions. The adapted idea is to apply this hierarchical clustering and far-field approximation framework to the dense kernel matrices arising from the discretization of high-dimensional integral equations. By organizing the discretization points in the high-dimensional domain into a tree structure (e.g., an octree for 3D) and approximating kernel interactions between well-separated clusters via low-rank factorizations or analytic expansions, the computational complexity of setting up and solving the system can be reduced from O(N²) to near O(N log N). This enables the feasible numerical solution of integral equations in dimensions where traditional methods would be computationally intractable.

ideas.title: Derive Complementary Variational Principles for Nonlinear Integral Equations via Thermodynamic Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution strategy from non-equilibrium thermodynamics, where complementary variational principles (e.g., the principles of minimum and maximum entropy production) provide dual bounding estimates for dissipative processes. The analogous solution involves identifying a pair of convex/concave functionals related via a Legendre transformation, whose stationarity conditions are equivalent to the governing equations. Adapted to the context of nonlinear integral equations, the goal is to systematically derive two complementary variational formulations: one primal functional (e.g., based on the solution function) and one dual functional (e.g., based on an adjoint or transformed field). These formulations will bound a key quantity of interest from above and below, ensuring the existence of solutions, enabling the development of robust error-controlled numerical methods (like mixed finite elements), and providing a deeper theoretical understanding of the problem's structure, similar to how thermodynamic potentials reveal stability criteria.

ideas.title: Adapting Weak Formulation and Regularization from Seismic Imaging to Integral Equations
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the classical smoothness assumptions on kernels and solutions in integral equations. Drawing an analogy from geophysical seismic imaging—where integral equations with discontinuous or singular kernels are routinely solved to image subsurface faults—we propose to adapt its solution strategy. In seismic imaging, the standard approach abandons point-wise formulations in favor of weak (variational) formulations combined with tailored regularization (e.g., Total Variation) that preserves discontinuities. The problem is discretized in functional spaces compatible with the expected solution regularity. We adapt this to develop a general framework for integral equations with discontinuous or distributional kernels. The adapted idea involves reformulating the integral equation in a weak sense, carefully selecting paired test and solution spaces (like Sobolev spaces with low regularity index or Besov spaces), and integrating discontinuity-preserving regularization directly into the solution method. The outcome will be new existence/uniqueness theories, stable discretization schemes (e.g., using discontinuous basis functions), and robust numerical solvers for a class of previously intractable non-smooth integral equations.

ideas.title: Dual variational-wavelet frameworks for nonlinear singular integral equations
ideas.description: This research synthesizes dual variational principles with wavelet Galerkin methods to develop a new computational theory for nonlinear integral equations with singular kernels. It derives complementary minimization and saddle-point principles tailored for equations involving singularities, ensuring solution existence and providing natural error measures. The wavelet discretization is then intrinsically matched to these principles, leveraging its multi-resolution structure to handle singular integrals accurately and to adaptively refine based on the variational error estimators. The result is a unified framework that yields provably convergent, adaptive algorithms with rigorous a posteriori error control for nonlinear singular problems.

ideas.title: Adapting Phase Transition Mapping from Statistical Physics to Kernel Bifurcation Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodology for analyzing phase transitions from statistical physics to create a general framework for finding bifurcation points in equations with non-polynomial kernels. In statistical physics, renormalization group (RG) methods and finite-size scaling are used to systematically identify critical points (phase transitions) by studying how a system's description changes under scale transformations and by looking for divergences in quantities like correlation length. The analogous solution involves coarse-graining the system to find fixed points in parameter space that mark the transitions. Adapted to the kernel context, this means developing a mathematical procedure to apply a similar scaling and coarse-graining transformation directly to the non-polynomial kernel function within an integral or integro-differential equation. The research would focus on defining a suitable RG flow for kernel spaces, identifying how solution branches evolve under this flow, and establishing that bifurcation points in the original system correspond to fixed points or instabilities within this transformed analysis. This provides a principled, systematic alternative to polynomial-based bifurcation theory, potentially applicable to exponential, logarithmic, fractional, or other complex kernel types.

ideas.title: Adapt VIM from Quantum Tunneling to Fractional Disease Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea proposes transferring the Variational Iteration Method (VIM) from its successful application in Quantum Mechanics—specifically for solving the fractional Schrödinger equation governing quantum tunneling through time-dependent barriers—to the domain of epidemiological modeling. The analogous solution involved constructing a correction functional with a Lagrange multiplier derived from variational principles for fractional calculus, which was then applied iteratively. This approach is adapted by developing a parallel VIM framework tailored for a system of fractional differential equations describing disease dynamics (e.g., a fractional SIR model). The adaptation requires defining an appropriate correction functional for the coupled system, determining the corresponding Lagrange multipliers for the Caputo fractional derivatives, and implementing an iterative algorithm to produce an analytical approximation for the compartmental populations over time, offering a new analytical tool for studying memory and non-local effects in disease spread.

ideas.description: This project develops a unified, open-source software package that implements advanced computational schemes for integral equations, specifically redesigned to leverage tensor train decompositions. The core innovation is the reformulation of integral operators and their discretizations within the TT format, enabling the manipulation of high-dimensional systems with near-linear scaling in the dimension. This synergy allows the software to tackle previously intractable problems in fields like quantum chemistry, kinetic theory, and uncertainty quantification, where the governing equations are high-dimensional integral equations. The resulting tool provides both the algorithmic robustness of established integral equation methods and the dimensionality-breaking power of modern tensor networks.

ideas.title: Tensor Train Methods for High-Dimensional Stochastic Integral Equations
ideas.description: This research direction develops a computational framework for solving high-dimensional Fredholm or Volterra-type integral equations where the kernel is a random field, making the solution a high-dimensional stochastic object. The core innovation is to represent the stochastic integral operator in a Tensor Train (TT) format, exploiting low-rank structures in both the spatial and stochastic domains. This allows for the compressed representation and iterative solution of the equation, making problems in uncertainty quantification, such as those with random media or forcing, tractable in dimensions previously considered infeasible. The synergy creates a new methodology that simultaneously addresses complexity from high-dimensional integration and from uncertainty propagation.

ideas.title: Spectral-Collocation Kernel Reconstruction for Volterra-Type Inverse Problems
ideas.description: This research direction develops a framework for solving kernel identification inverse problems by discretizing the governing integral equation using high-order spectral collocation methods. The spectral accuracy allows for the precise representation of both smooth and challenging kernel structures, while the collocation formulation naturally provides a discrete linear system for the unknown kernel values at spectral nodes. By combining this with regularization techniques tailored to the spectral basis, the method can efficiently reconstruct kernels from limited and noisy solution data with quantified uncertainty, directly leveraging the convergence properties of spectral methods to mitigate the inherent ill-posedness of the inverse problem.

ideas.title: Stochastic Integral Equations via Quantum Field Theory Path Integral Methods
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to generalize the deterministic framework of integral equations to include random coefficients or forcing terms. By drawing an analogy from Quantum Field Theory, the problem is re-conceptualized using the path integral method. In the analogous QFT solution, systems with inherent randomness (quantum fluctuations) are solved by constructing a generating functional that integrates over all possible configurations. This approach is adapted to stochastic integral equations by treating the randomness as a field and formulating the solution's expectation as a similar functional integral. The adapted idea involves developing a 'stochastic path integral' formalism, enabling the use of perturbative diagrammatic techniques and renormalization group analysis to obtain statistical properties of solutions, such as moments and correlation functions, thereby providing a powerful analytical and computational toolkit for this challenging class of equations.

ideas.title: Multi-Scale Stochastic Integral Equations: Analysis and Homogenization for Random Media
ideas.description: This research direction develops a unified analytical and computational framework for integral equations where the kernel is both stochastic and exhibits multiple, widely separated scales (e.g., random microstructures). The core challenge is to characterize how randomness interacts with scale separation to influence the statistical properties and large-scale effective behavior of the solution. The work will focus on proving homogenization and averaging limits for such equations, deriving explicit effective kernels or stochastic partial differential equations that capture the macroscopic system behavior. This provides a foundational tool for uncertainty quantification in heterogeneous materials, wave propagation in random media, and multiscale biological systems where randomness is inherent.

ideas.title: Network-Adaptive Hybrid Solvers for Singular Integro-Differential Models of Onchocerciasis Transmission
ideas.description: This research develops novel hybrid numerical-analytical methods specifically designed to solve the singular integral equations that arise when modeling river blindness (onchocerciasis) transmission on heterogeneous spatial or contact networks. The core challenge is that the kernel of the integral equation, representing contact and transmission probability, becomes strongly singular due to the complex, short-range interactions inherent in a realistic network structure. By fusing network-based epidemiological modeling with advanced schemes for singular integrals—such as combining singularity subtraction, quadrature on adapted meshes, and fast multipole methods—we create solvers that can compute key quantities like the reproduction number and endemic equilibrium directly from the integral formulation, without resorting to massive stochastic simulation. This enables the rigorous study of how network topology influences disease persistence and control strategy efficacy for a neglected tropical disease governed by a complex transmission cycle.

ideas.title: Create an Integral Equation Solver Framework with a Modular, Community-Driven Architecture
ideas.description: The original idea is to implement computational schemes from a book into a unified, open-source software package for solving integral equations. Drawing an analogy from computational quantum chemistry, specifically the successful development of the Psi4 package, which unified fragmented research codes into a modular, community-driven framework. The analogous solution involved creating a core infrastructure with standardized APIs and a plugin system, allowing diverse methods to coexist and be easily compared. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to create an integral equation solver framework with a modular architecture. It would consist of a robust core library handling common low-level tasks and a well-defined API, enabling researchers to contribute specific discretization schemes and solvers from the literature as compatible modules. This approach transforms the software from a static implementation of one book's methods into a living, extensible platform that accelerates development, ensures reproducibility, and facilitates direct comparison of different integral equation techniques within a unified environment.

The core innovation is to embed control parameters directly into the homotopy series solution of a nonlinear integral equation model (e.g., for malaria or neglected tropical diseases). This allows for the analytical exploration of the solution manifold with respect to control variables, facilitating the direct calculation of sensitivity and the systematic derivation of optimal or near-optimal intervention schedules. The synergy provides a powerful, semi-analytical tool for designing and validating time-dependent control policies that are inherently linked to the model's nonlinear dynamics.

It specifies necessary and sufficient conditions on the kernel's randomness (e.g., mean-square continuity, Carathéodory-type measurability) and the driving noise to guarantee the existence and uniqueness of solutions via a stochastic Picard iteration. The approach then systematically analyzes the solution's statistical properties, such as moment bounds and continuity, and provides a foundation for developing convergent numerical methods, like stochastic Galerkin or Monte Carlo quadrature, with proven error estimates.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Signal Denoising for Singular Equation Solutions
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, specifically seismic signal denoising, to the context of using wavelet Galerkin methods for solving singular equations. In seismic processing, wavelet transforms are used to denoise data by isolating singular signal features (like fault reflections) from background noise through thresholding in the wavelet domain. The adapted research idea applies this principle of multi-scale isolation and selective coefficient treatment to numerical analysis. Within the wavelet Galerkin framework for singular equations, we propose developing an adaptive scheme that identifies wavelet coefficients corresponding to solution singularities and multi-scale features. This scheme, inspired by denoising thresholding, would then apply specialized treatment (e.g., tailored integration, local refinement, or preconditioning) to these critical coefficients to handle singularities more effectively, leading to more accurate and stable solutions compared to standard wavelet Galerkin approaches.

ideas.title: Import Chebyshev spectral methods from quantum chemistry for integral operators
ideas.description: The original idea proposes importing high-order spectral methods from PDEs for discretizing integral operators. By analogy, the field of quantum chemistry has long faced a similar challenge: accurately computing integrals with singular kernels (like the Coulomb potential) over infinite or semi-infinite domains for electronic structure calculations. The analogous solution employs spectral methods, particularly using Chebyshev polynomial expansions on mapped, non-uniform grids (like a Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto grid mapped to [0, ∞)), to achieve exponential convergence and handle singularities. Adapting this solution, we propose using Chebyshev spectral collocation methods specifically tailored for integral operators. The integration variable is discretized on a Chebyshev grid, often with a coordinate mapping to handle infinite limits or boundary layers in the kernel. This approach yields a dense spectral differentiation/integration matrix for the integral operator, providing a high-order accurate discretization that is especially effective for smooth kernels and can be combined with fast summation techniques for practical implementation.

It focuses on creating scalable hierarchical methods that dynamically learn efficient kernel approximations—such as data-driven partitioned low-rank formats or neural operator-inspired compressions—directly within the distributed-memory environment of exascale systems. The core synergy lies in tightly coupling the architectural constraints and parallelism of modern supercomputers with the adaptive numerical analysis required for these complex kernels, enabling previously intractable simulations. This will allow for high-fidelity modeling of multi-physics phenomena with long-range, heterogeneous interactions across unprecedented problem scales.

ideas.title: High-dimensional integral equation models for onchocerciasis transmission on spatial contact networks
ideas.description: This research direction develops and applies advanced computational schemes for solving high-dimensional (3D+) integral equations to model river blindness (onchocerciasis) transmission on explicit spatial or contact networks. By embedding the network's adjacency or distance-based contact kernel directly into the integral operator, the method relaxes the homogeneous mixing assumption at a fundamental mathematical level, preserving the exact structure of heterogeneous contacts. This synergy allows for the precise calculation of invasion thresholds, endemic equilibria, and control intervention impacts in complex, realistic landscapes where transmission risk is driven by local riverine geography and human movement patterns, which was previously intractable for high-dimensional spatial models.

ideas.title: Adaptive Mesh Refinement for Real-Time Integral Equation Solvers
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution strategy of Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) from Computational Fluid Dynamics to the challenge of creating lightweight, fast solvers for integral equations in real-time embedded control systems. In CFD, AMR solves large-scale PDEs efficiently by dynamically refining the computational mesh only in regions with high solution gradients, coarsening it elsewhere to save resources. The adapted idea is to create an integral equation solver that employs an analogous 'adaptive discretization' strategy. The solver would dynamically and sparingly allocate high-resolution basis functions or collocation points to critical parts of the integral equation's domain—such as the most recent history in a control loop's memory integral or regions near system constraints—while using a much coarser, cheaper representation for less critical segments. This results in a miniaturized, variable-fidelity solver capable of meeting real-time deadlines on embedded hardware without sacrificing control performance where it is most needed.
