ideas.title: Real-Time, Lightweight Solvers for Integral Equations on Unbounded Domains in Embedded Control
ideas.description: This research fuses techniques for solving equations on unbounded/complex domains with the need for miniaturized, real-time solvers for embedded control. It develops novel, ultra-lightweight numerical methods and approximations specifically for integral equations defined on infinite or semi-infinite domains, such as those arising in predictive control with infinite horizons or field estimation in unbounded environments. The core innovation is a co-design of algorithm and hardware that strategically truncates, transforms, or compresses the domain representation to achieve bounded computational cost while rigorously preserving stability guarantees for the feedback loop. This enables advanced, model-based control strategies for autonomous systems operating in open environments to run on microcontrollers or low-power FPGAs.

ideas.title: Inferring the Interaction Law from Collective Behavior Data
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a core inverse problem in collective behavior physics—inferring the interaction law from observed group dynamics—to the mathematical context of kernel identification. In collective behavior, the 'kernel' is the interaction rule (e.g., how force depends on distance between agents), and it is recovered by fitting a parameterized model to trajectory data. The adapted research idea applies this methodology to determine an unknown kernel function in an integral or differential equation from observed solution data. By assuming a flexible, parameterized representation for the kernel (e.g., in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space or via neural networks) and setting up a variational or Bayesian inference framework that penalizes mismatch with data and possibly kernel smoothness, one can computationally solve the inverse problem. This transforms kernel identification into a high-dimensional optimization or inference task, directly parallel to how interaction potentials are learned from the 'solution data' of collective motion.

ideas.title: Adaptive Goal-Oriented Wavelet Schemes for Singular PDEs with Unknown Singular Support
ideas.description: This method combines the multi-resolution analysis of wavelets with a robust adaptive refinement strategy that does not require prior knowledge of singularity locations. It employs a solver for a dual (adjoint) problem to compute a posteriori error indicators that guide wavelet selection and grid refinement specifically for quantities of interest. This results in a computationally efficient, self-tuning algorithm that automatically concentrates resolution near singularities and complex features, ensuring optimal convergence rates even for problems with hidden or irregular singular supports. The approach is rigorously justified through nonlinear approximation theory, guaranteeing near-best N-term approximation for the targeted output functional.

ideas.title: Multi-Scale Optimal Control for Disease Management Using Adaptive Wavelet Discretization
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel computational framework for optimal disease control by integrating wavelet-Galerkin discretization with dynamic intervention strategies. The wavelet basis naturally handles the multi-scale, singular features of disease transmission (such as localized outbreaks or sharp spatial gradients) within the model's state equations. This accurate representation is then directly coupled to a control-theoretic formulation, allowing for the optimization of intervention parameters (e.g., drug administration schedules, resource allocation for vector control) that are specifically adapted to these resolved fine-scale dynamics. The result is a high-fidelity control system that can design strategies which are both globally optimal and precisely targeted to mitigate critical local singularities in the epidemic wave.

ideas.title: Real-Time Embedded Control with Non-Smooth and Impulsive Kernels
ideas.description: This research develops ultra-lightweight, fixed-complexity numerical solvers for integral equations with discontinuous or distributional kernels, specifically designed for deployment in microcontrollers and FPGAs within real-time control loops. The core innovation is a method that leverages the structure of non-smooth kernels (e.g., from Coulomb friction, relay switches, or impulsive disturbances) to create highly efficient, approximate solvers that meet strict timing deadlines. This fusion directly addresses control scenarios involving contact, saturation, or digital actuation that are intractable for traditional smooth-kernel algorithms on embedded platforms, enabling a new class of robust, low-cost embedded controllers for advanced mechatronics and robotics.

ideas.title: Real-Time Embedded Control via Compressed Wavelet Integral Equation Solvers
ideas.description: This research develops ultra-lightweight, fast solvers for integral equations by leveraging the inherent compression and multi-resolution properties of wavelet bases within a Galerkin framework, specifically optimized for embedded system control feedback loops. The core innovation is a solver architecture that exploits wavelet sparsity to achieve real-time performance while accurately capturing singularities and sharp transitions in system dynamics that standard basis functions miss. This enables high-fidelity control of complex physical systems with discontinuous or multi-scale features, such as robotic systems with contact or smart structures with damage, on low-power, memory-limited processors. The result is a new class of embedded predictive controllers that are both computationally tractable and mathematically robust for challenging real-world environments.

ideas.title: Multi-Scale Stochastic Integral Equations for Spatially Heterogeneous Epidemic Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel class of stochastic multi-scale integral equations to model disease transmission. The kernel of the integral equation incorporates separated spatial and temporal scales—such as fast, local contact dynamics within households and slow, global mobility patterns—while the stochasticity is embedded to capture random outbreak ignition and superspreading events at the finest scale. This framework allows for the derivation of asymptotic approximations that explicitly connect the probability distributions of micro-scale random events to the emergent macro-scale prevalence and wave patterns. Consequently, it provides a mathematically rigorous tool to design targeted interventions that disrupt the critical pathways through which randomness propagates across scales to cause large outbreaks.

ideas.title: Neural Network Kernel Learning via Computational Chemistry Force Field Parameterization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from computational chemistry, where machine learning models have been used to learn complex, data-driven potential energy surfaces that replace rigid empirical force fields. The analogous problem in kernel methods is the limitation of pre-defined kernel functions with fixed parametric forms. The adapted research idea proposes using a neural network to learn optimal kernel representations directly from data, treating the kernel as a learnable similarity function. This approach would allow the discovery of task-specific, potentially non-stationary and non-Mercer kernels that are more expressive than classical kernels, effectively parameterizing the 'interaction' between data points in a latent space in a purely data-driven manner, similar to how neural network potentials learn atomic interactions from quantum mechanical data.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Physics-Informed Tensor Train Decomposition with Hybrid Rank Refinement for High-Dimensional Systems
ideas.description: This research develops an adaptive tensor train (TT) decomposition framework that overcomes the fundamental limitation of fixed low-rank approximations for complex high-dimensional systems. The system integrates physics-based error estimators and sensitivity analysis to dynamically identify regions in parameter or state space where the TT representation is insufficient. It then performs targeted rank augmentation or employs a hybrid TT-local model only in these critical subspaces, ensuring accuracy where needed while preserving efficiency elsewhere. This results in a robust, self-correcting method that mitigates the curse of dimensionality without relying on globally low-rank assumptions.

ideas.title: Adapting Blind Source Separation for Non-Separable Kernel Decomposition
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the Blind Source Separation problem from signal processing to address the challenge of non-separable kernels in machine learning. In BSS, methods like ICA solve the 'Cocktail Party Problem' by iteratively estimating both unknown source signals and an unknown mixing matrix, relying on statistical properties like independence rather than prior structural knowledge. The adapted idea is to treat a non-separable kernel function as an unknown mixture (or superposition) of latent, simpler kernel components. The research would create methods that jointly and iteratively learn a decomposition of the kernel (analogous to the mixing matrix) and the primary model parameters, using constraints such as sparsity, smoothness, or statistical independence among the latent components to guide the separation where standard decomposition assumptions fail.

ideas.title: Apply Hierarchical Matrix Compression from Computational Physics to Tensor Train Decompositions
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using tensor train (TT) decompositions to mitigate the curse of dimensionality. By analogy, the field of computational physics long faced a similar 'dense matrix curse' when solving integral equations via Boundary Element Methods. The analogous solution was Hierarchical Matrix (H-matrix) compression, which uses a tree structure to identify and compress low-rank off-diagonal blocks. Adapting this, we propose a Hierarchical Tensor Train (HTT) format. This hybrid method applies hierarchical, block-wise low-rank approximation within the individual cores of a TT decomposition, specifically targeting the compression of interactions corresponding to weakly coupled dimensions or long-range correlations. This adaptation aims to achieve super-compression for very high-dimensional systems where standard TT ranks would still be prohibitively large, enabling more efficient storage and computation for complex systems in quantum chemistry, stochastic PDEs, or machine learning.

ideas.title: Robust Stability and Resilience Analysis for Epidemic Models under Uncertainty and Heterogeneity
ideas.description: This research develops a framework that extends a complete analytical stability analysis by rigorously incorporating real-world complexities. It quantifies how parameter uncertainty, stochastic forces, and population heterogeneity affect the stability and resilience of disease model equilibria. The approach combines Lyapunov-based methods with global sensitivity analysis and stochastic simulations to produce probabilistic stability assessments. The final output is a practical toolkit that maps stability regions to actionable public health parameters, directly informing intervention strategies under uncertainty.

ideas.title: Communication-Avoiding and Memory-Centric Co-Design for Integral Equations on Exascale Heterogeneous Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a holistic framework for scaling integral equation solvers by treating data movement as a first-class constraint. It creates a library of communication-avoiding algorithmic variants and a predictive performance model that maps computation patterns to optimal data layouts and task schedules for a target architecture. The system automatically generates and tunes architecture-specific kernels, ensuring that parallel efficiency is sustained by minimizing off-chip data transfer rather than just maximizing floating-point operations. This results in robust, portable performance across diverse CPU-GPU and many-core systems, directly addressing the primary limiter of large-scale scientific computing.

ideas.title: Robust Characterization of Kernel Equivalence Classes from Noisy Observational Data
ideas.description: This research addresses the fundamental ill-posedness of kernel identification by developing a framework to characterize the entire set of kernels compatible with observed solutions, rather than seeking a single, potentially non-existent, unique solution. The system integrates physical priors and regularization not as strict constraints, but as tools to define a preferred, optimally regular representative from the admissible equivalence class. It outputs both this representative kernel and a quantitative measure of the remaining uncertainty or ambiguity in the identification. This approach provides a rigorous and practically useful answer even when perfect inversion is theoretically impossible.

ideas.title: Calibrate river blindness model using data assimilation techniques from climate science
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes to solve the model validation challenge by drawing an analogy from climate science. The analogous problem is the calibration of complex, imperfect numerical climate models using sparse real-world data, which is solved through data assimilation techniques like the Ensemble Kalman Filter. The adapted idea applies this solution to the river blindness context: instead of a one-time fitting, implement a data assimilation framework that continuously integrates actual, sparse case data from affected regions (e.g., annual prevalence surveys, treatment coverage data) to optimally adjust the model's internal parameters and state estimates. This process will dynamically calibrate the model against real epidemiological trends, reduce uncertainty, and produce a more robust and validated tool for forecasting the impact of mass drug administration and other intervention strategies.

ideas.title: Real-Time Stability Certification for Embedded Epidemic Control Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for embedding a complete Lyapunov-based stability analysis of an extended compartmental disease model directly into a resource-constrained microcontroller. The core challenge is to miniaturize the computational routines for equilibrium point analysis and Lyapunov function evaluation into ultra-fast, lightweight solvers that fit within a real-time control feedback loop. This enables an embedded device, such as a smart environmental controller or a wearable health monitor, to not just implement a pre-defined policy but to continuously certify the local stability of the current disease state and adjust its intervention parameters (e.g., air flow, isolation signals) accordingly. The result is a new class of provably stable, adaptive, and model-predictive epidemic control systems that operate autonomously at the edge.

ideas.title: Robust Adaptive Lightweight Solvers for Embedded Control under Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research develops lightweight integral equation solvers specifically designed for robustness in embedded control feedback loops. The core innovation is a modular architecture pairing a fast, minimal-complexity nominal solver with a meta-adaptation layer that monitors closed-loop performance. This adaptation layer uses efficient online data to tune solver parameters or switch between pre-validated simplified models, ensuring stability despite disturbances and model errors. The result is a real-time capable system that provides formal robustness certificates, making it suitable for safety-critical embedded applications where both computational resources and operational reliability are constrained.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Kernel Learning for Spatially-Explicit River Blindness Model Calibration
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using neural networks to learn the optimal spatial transmission kernel for river blindness directly from historical and contemporary case data across diverse endemic regions. Instead of calibrating pre-defined kernel parameters, the machine learning model will infer the functional form and scale of human-to-blackfly and blackfly-to-human transmission from the complex spatial patterns in the data. This data-driven kernel can then be integrated into the mechanistic epidemiological model, creating a hybrid framework that is both interpretable and precisely tailored to real transmission dynamics. This approach can reveal region-specific ecological and environmental drivers of spread that are not captured by standard theoretical kernels.

ideas.title: Adapting Phenological Models from Plant Ecology to Predict Blackfly Vector Activity
ideas.description: This idea challenges the constant parameter assumption in disease modeling by drawing an analogy from plant ecology. In that field, phenological models successfully predict seasonal biological events by integrating environmental drivers. The analogous solution is adapted by using similar mechanistic modeling (e.g., thermal accumulation for larval development, rainfall thresholds for breeding habitat) to create a time-dependent function for blackfly vector activity and disease transmission rates. This replaces a fixed seasonal parameter with a dynamic one derived from measurable environmental data, improving model realism and predictive power for onchocerciasis and other vector-borne diseases.

ideas.title: A Data-Assimilation Framework for Validating and Integrating Age-Dependent Exposure in Onchocerciasis Models
ideas.description: This research develops a modeling framework that formally tests and integrates age-dependent factors based on empirical validation. It begins by constructing a suite of candidate demographic modules (e.g., age-dependent biting exposure, age-specific MDA coverage) and uses Bayesian inference with historical epidemiological and programmatic data from specific foci to determine which structures are necessary and well-supported. The validated module is then coupled to the dynamic transmission model, ensuring that the added complexity directly improves predictive accuracy for key outcomes like time-to-elimination and the impact of alternative treatment strategies.

ideas.title: Adapting Optimal Control Theory from Engineering to Model Intervention Strategies
ideas.description: This idea proposes extending the epidemiological model by incorporating principles from Control Systems Engineering, specifically optimal control theory. The analogous engineering solution involves modeling a dynamic system and computing control inputs to optimize a performance metric. Adapted to the research context, the epidemiological model (e.g., tracking susceptible, infected, and recovered populations) becomes the dynamic system. Control variables are defined for each intervention strategy (e.g., a time-varying rate for drug administration coverage or vector control effort). A cost function is formulated that includes the economic cost of implementing these controls and a measure of disease burden (e.g., number of infections or deaths). Using optimal control theory, the model can then be solved to identify the precise, time-dependent application of interventions that would most efficiently drive the disease system toward a desired state, such as elimination or sustained low prevalence, providing a mathematically rigorous framework for planning and optimizing public health campaigns.

ideas.title: Provably Valid and Generalizable Neural Kernel Learning with Constrained Meta-Architectures
ideas.description: This research designs a neural network-based framework that learns adaptive kernel functions from data while guaranteeing their mathematical validity and generalization. The core innovation is a meta-architecture that uses neural networks to generate kernel parameters but processes them through a constrained, differentiable layer ensuring the final kernel is positive semi-definite. The training objective combines data fidelity with regularization terms that promote alignment with known task structures and smoothness. This approach yields kernels that retain the flexibility of learned representations while providing the theoretical robustness and interpretability of classical kernel methods.

ideas.title: Adversarial Meta-Learning for Kernel Structure Discovery
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to automatically discover efficient, approximate decompositions for kernels where standard separability fails. A hypernetwork proposes candidate kernel structures, which are then evaluated and refined through a bi-level optimization process that pits a structure proposer against a kernel learner. The system outputs not only a performant kernel but also a certificate—either a computationally efficient quasi-separable representation or evidence that such a representation is unlikely to exist for the given task, providing actionable insight.

ideas.title: Data-Adaptive Stochastic Filtering for Plausible Outbreak Pathway Projection
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simple stochastic simulation by embedding it within a real-time data assimilation framework. The system generates an ensemble of stochastic outbreak trajectories but continuously weights and resamples them based on their fit to incoming epidemiological data, such as case counts and genomic sequences. This filters out highly improbable random paths and concentrates probability mass on the subset of stochastic realizations that are consistent with observations. The output is not just a cloud of uncertainty but a refined set of plausible future scenarios with associated probabilities, directly supporting targeted intervention planning.

ideas.title: Multi-Fidelity Bayesian Validation Framework for River Blindness Models Under Extreme Data Scarcity
ideas.description: This research designs a validation system for river blindness models that explicitly confronts the reality of sparse, heterogeneous, and incomplete real-world data. It employs a Bayesian hierarchical framework to jointly calibrate the model against all available data sources—aggregated historical prevalence, geolocated cross-sectional surveys, and entomological indices—while rigorously quantifying parameter and structural uncertainty. The approach includes stress-testing model projections against competing epidemiological hypotheses and simulated data gaps to evaluate robustness. The outcome is a validated model accompanied by a transparent map of its uncertainties and the specific conditions under which its predictions are most reliable for policy planning.

ideas.title: Empirically-Grounded, Policy-Relevant Heterogeneity in River Blindness Transmission Models
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simply adding structure by embedding empirical data collection into the modeling process to define and validate heterogeneous contact patterns. It focuses on gathering field data on human mobility, water contact behaviors, and blackfly flight ranges to parameterize network or spatial models realistically. The core innovation is a sequential framework where model complexity is increased only if it demonstrably improves the accuracy of key policy predictions, such as the required coverage for elimination or the optimal placement of vector control. This ensures the final model is both scientifically robust and directly useful for program managers by quantifying the practical impact of heterogeneity on intervention success.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Adaptive Control via Learned Kernel Operators for Epidemiological Interventions
ideas.description: This research fuses kernel approximation via neural networks with epidemiological control strategies to create a novel adaptive intervention framework. The core idea is to train a neural network to learn a kernel operator that maps the state of a disease system (e.g., infection prevalence, vector density) directly to an optimal, multi-dimensional control action (e.g., timing, location, and dosage of interventions). This learned kernel captures complex, non-linear interactions in the data that traditional mechanistic models might miss, allowing the control policy to adapt to local conditions and unexpected transmission patterns. The result is a closed-loop, data-driven control system that can recommend personalized, context-aware intervention strategies, potentially improving efficacy and resource allocation for public health campaigns.

papers.title: A First Course in Integral Equations
papers.abstract:
Partial Differential Equations (PDE) are one of the topics where Engineering students
find more difficulties when facing Math subjects.
A basic course in Partial Differential Equations (PDE) in Engineering, usually deals at
